### Post by zajc on 2011-06-17
I installed Xubuntu 11.04 with Alternate Install CD on HP Pavilion G7-1007EM notebook. Everything worked well until yesterday I forcibly powered off the machine. After that I can not get into X session or get into text terminal. The screen is black, keyboard is not working and the fan is spinning like crazy. I tried all the switches in the grub startup you proposed and nothing works. I also renamed xorg.conf to xorg.old (with help of Ubuntu 10.10 Live CD). I made Xubuntu 11.04 Desktop Live CD and it also doesn't work. Black screen, no mater which switches were used. I create Ubuntu 10.10 Desktop Live CD and it works.

I think downgrading kernel will help. **Is there any recipe how to downgrade the kernel in my situation without text terminal?** I will downgrade to Xubuntu 10.10 if I don't find any solution.

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-06-17
> **zajc said:**
> I installed Xubuntu 11.04 with Alternate Install CD on HP Pavilion G7-1007EM notebook. Everything worked well until yesterday I forcibly powered off the machine. After that I can not get into X session or get into text terminal. The screen is black, keyboard is not working and the fan is spinning like crazy. I tried all the switches in the grub startup you proposed and nothing works. I also renamed xorg.conf to xorg.old (with help of Ubuntu 10.10 Live CD). I made Xubuntu 11.04 Desktop Live CD and it also doesn't work. Black screen, no mater which switches were used. I create Ubuntu 10.10 Desktop Live CD and it works.

I think downgrading kernel will help. **Is there any recipe how to downgrade the kernel in my situation without text terminal?** I will downgrade to Xubuntu 10.10 if I don't find any solution.
My condolences.  Your laptop hae an AMD Radeon HD 6470M video chipset that is behind a switched Intel video chipset.  Here is what ATI says about it's own Linux support of the 6xxxs series chipsets:
> 
_**Switchable Graphic Chips Warning**_

    [SIZE=-1]**WARNING**[/SIZE]    
As of AMD Catalyst&#8482; 10.12, there is no support for switchable  graphics chips in Linux. This means you cannot switch between the low  power consumption of the integrated graphics chip and the high  performance of the discrete graphics chip. Some manufacturers allow the  IGP to be turned off in the BIOS and use the discrete card only (but  this is not good for battery life). Otherwise, you are stuck with both  GPU's turned on and draining the battery while only being able to use  the IGP. Carefully research before purchasing a laptop, or you may not  be able to fully use the hardware you pay for.

  There is a bug raised against the inability to [switch between integrated and discrete graphics cards]("http://ati.cchtml.com/show_bug.cgi?id=16") ATI's own disclaimer is sort of misleading.  They have been trying to support this in Linux.  There is also work on this in this community.  There is hope.  Unfortunately, your idea on downgrading the kernel or to downgrade versions to 10.10 doesn't seem to be some of the viable answers for this chipset- as I see the same problems in 10.10 (as 11.04) with the ATI 6xxx chipsets.  On ones that "are" working, there still seems to be an annoyance where there will be a "watermark" on the screen that says "[AMD Unsupported Hardware]("http://wiki.cchtml.com/index.php/Ubuntu#Unsupported_adapter")" in the lower right of the screen.

The reason it comes up successfully with 10,10 and 11,04 LiveCD's is that it is coming up with the graphics of the Intel Video Chipset and not natively with the ATI Graphics Chipset.  That limits your resolutions, video performance and such to the low-graphics mode of the Intel chipset..  It see's the first chipset and not what is beyond it...

Now that I've given my little disclaimer above... Let's jump in to this and see if we can get you going again... Once we do that, then we'll retrace ground others have broken and see if we get get any further with this.  No promises, but at least we'll try right?  If not better, then at least as well as what is in current workarounds.

First we need to get you "running" with a "usable" laptop.  Remove your power cord and battery.  Press and hold your power button for 30 seconds.  Replace your battery and power cord.  On initial boot up, go into your BIOS setup menu > find the BIOS option to switch your switchable video graphics to the Intel Chipset.  Save and Exit. Continue Boot.  As long as you still have your xorg.conf renamed to xorg.old and there is no xorg.conf present, it should be able to boot with a graphical X-session.  

If your BIOS does not have an option in it's BIOS Setup Menu to switch between chipsets, then tell me.  There may be a kernel mechanism that may allow you to switch between GPUs.

Before we start making changes, we need a snapshot of what you have and what is  going on.  Please open a terminal and execute the following commands (individually), then post the results:
```

sudo lspci -vv | grep VGA

grep -i switcheroo /boot/config-2.6.*

sudo hwinfo --framebuffer

sudo hwinfo --monitor

xrandr -q

lsb_release -sd || cat /etc/*release &&  uname -s -r &&  unity --version && /usr/lib/nux/unity_support_test -p

glxgears

dpkg --get-selections | grep fglrx*

dpkg --get-selections | grep xserver-xorg*


```Which will give us the info on your system, video card and monitor as 11.04 see's them.  

If hwinfo returns a "command not found" please install via 
```

sudo apt-get install hwinfo

```And run those commands again to post the info from above.

The next 2 files to post are:
```

/etc/X!!/xorg.old  # You said you renamed your xorg.conf to this name.
/var/log/xorg.0.log

```That will tell us an idea on what drivers you are using, how they are configured and what modules are being loaded...

After that... (credit [3602]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=1215192") and updates from him on 2011.25.07 are now included), ATI and 3 other sources-- edited, translated, cleaned up and adapted from those instructions) 
**[COLOR=Red]You might want to print or copy these instructions down before you go on.[/COLOR]**  You are going to reboot a couple times and you might want to have these instructions handy during all that...

[SIZE=4]_**/* --- Radeon Binary Driver Install --- */**_[/SIZE]
_Step 1:_
Create an "ATI_Drivers" directory in your download directory to download/store your ATI driver in...  Example:
```

mkdir ~/Downloads/ATI_Drivers/

```_Step 2:_
Download Catalyst v11.x at this link: [_ATI_]("http://support.amd.com/us/gpudownload/Pages/index.aspx").  
/*  
Recommended Version
  - Note: 11.10 works, 11.9 does not work with unity/compiz, 11.8 works... 
*/
Put into the directory you created above: ~/Downloads/ATI_Drivers/

_Step 3:_
```
sudo  rm /usr/share/ati/fglrx-uninstall.sh
```If this returns "no such file or directory" it's fine. Just means that that is not present, but we need to make sure it's gone before we reinstall. Proceed.

_Step 4:_
```
sudo apt-get remove --purge fglrx*
```This will *probably* return a couple of warnings: */usr/share/lib/fglrx* and */usr/share/lib32/fglrx *not empty. If so. then do:
```
sudo rm -rf /usr/share/lib/fglrx
```and
```
sudo rm -rf /usr/share/lib32/fglrx
```> " Quote from an update from [3602]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=1215192"):
In step Four, there is a possibility that the announced "not empty"  directories are not the ones listed. When I tried to install Catalyst  11.6 yesterday, the removal of Catalyst 11.5 prompted *four* "not-empty" directories that are *not* the ones listed. After an execution of *rm -rf* commands, I got *kernel panics*. I don't know whether this is an isolated case, but I sure hope so.

Also after step Four, there is a possibility that apt-get announces DKMS  is not needed anymore since the FGLRX DKMS modules are removed. This  only happens if the only DKMS modules are of the FGLRX, but if one is to  remove DKMS as per apt-get's suggestion, the later FGLRX installation  cannot proceed (since no DKMS modules will be able to install without  the DKMS itself).

So DKMS is not to be removed even if apt-get announces so.
Proceed.
[U]
Step 5:.[/U][B] 
[COLOR=Red]COPY THIS DOWN on some papers before doing it![/COLOR][/B]
Reboot. When in the purple boot screen with flashing dots, press F1 key  to get into the emergency recovery board (white-on-black screen). Do:
```
sudo mv /etc/X11/xorg.conf /etc/X11/xorg.conf.old
```Then 
```
sudo reboot
```You will get a GUI.
[U]
Step 6:[/U]
*cd* into whatever directory you have the .run file in.
```

cd ~/Downloads/ATI_Drivers/

```You might want/need to ensure you have all the basic to do this:
```
[FONT=monospace]
[/FONT]sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install build-essential cdbs fakeroot dh-make debhelper  debconf libstdc++6 dkms libqtgui4 wget execstack libelfg0 dh-modaliases 

```If you are using 64bit OS, be sure to inslude ia32-libs before proceeding.```

sudo apt-get install ia32-libs 


```> Quote from [3602]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=1215192") before next step:
Also there should be no need in *chmodding* the file... But, better safe than sorry, I guess.Then build the debian packages.  Look to where is says [COLOR=Red]natty[/COLOR] below in red, if you are installing for onieric, replaced that with [COLOR=Red]oneiric[/COLOR]. Meaning that you have to tell it what distro and version of to build the debian packages for!
```

sudo chmod +x (your_Installation_filename).run
sudo sh (your_installation_filename).run --buildpkg  Ubuntu/[COLOR=Red]natty[/COLOR]
```Wait. A Synaptic window might pop up if you're doing  this for the first time. Let it go away on it's own. 
Later you'll see the xterm returning four packages built, similar to: 
```

fglrx_(version_number)-0ubuntu1_(platform).deb, 
fglrx-amdcccle_(version_number)-0ubuntu1_(platform).deb, 
fglrx-dev_(version_number)-0ubuntu1_(platform).deb, 
fglrx-modaliases_(version)number)-0ubuntu1_(platform).deb

```Now
```
sudo dpkg -i *.deb
```Wait.

Generate a generic Config...
_Step 7:_
```
sudo aticonfig --initial -f
```If you have dual monitors or have an X2 card (e.g. 4870X2 or 5970)
```

sudo aticonfig --initial -f
sudo aticonfig --set-pcs-str="DDX,EnableRandR12,FALSE"

```Then
```
sudo reboot
```then test your install
```
fglrxinfo
```If the last command returns "MESA" then for some reason it is still not using the ATI driver and there would be further instructions...  One of which is redoing all the above steps.

You can also test 
```
 
fgl_glxgears

```If you experience issues or a hang, you may need to disable fast TLS.

[code]

sudo aticonfig --tls=0

[code]


If you have Switchable graphics, it should work now. "fglxinfo" should show the intel driver in use.  You should then be able to switch to the ATI Radeon driver.

If you have any problems playing flash files in FireFox after installing this driver, you may have to add/install the FireFox [Flash-Aid]("https://addons.mozilla.org/en-US/firefox/addon/flash-aid/") extension.  On onieric, there is now an experemental hardware video decode accelration... only supposed to work for  newer RadeonHD GPU's (those with UVD2). If you have a RadeonHD 4000-series or newer, you have UVD2... Message me with your card model and I'll message back with the package names.

### Post by MYCALL on 2011-06-17
Yes bro...i got blank screen too..indicated that wubildr is missing....how to resolve Bro?

---

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-06-17
> **MYCALL said:**
> Yes bro...i got blank screen too..indicated that wubildr is missing....how to resolve Bro?
Welcome to the Ubuntu Forums!

Your blank screen does not seem to be caused by a Graphics Problem-  "wubildr.mbr is corrupt or missing" is a common WUBI boot problem... Which also means that you are running a form of Windows and Ubuntu in the same partition via WUBI.  This has a lot to to do with a particular boot file and what slammed it...  

The place you really want to look for current solutions to this and/or ask about this is at this link: "[The WUBI Megathread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1639198")"

---

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-06-17
> **johnthei said:**
> I will try again this weekend. How do the ubuntu development group find out about these problems and come up with plans to fix them? this seems to be a user group and few very dedicated users who are trying to help everyone.
I'm happy to see you back at this!!!  I will be ready to help you through this.

I don't know about others there... I feel lucky to test the development builds.  As someone there put it // sort of sum's it all up: "WOOT! Let The Breakage Begin!"  

We sure get to see things break (in a bad way) before others are exposed to them... Hopefully so others will not have to be exposed to them.  We get to come up with solutions on our own and see other's solutions to things.  We have to be involved with Launchpad in reporting bugs and trying LP fixes.

I go a little further by following graphics issues and fixes in Xorg, the development builds, the ubuntu-xswat and xorg-edger PPA's, Launchpad and the NVidia and ATI boards. I get a little different perspective on this as I also test various other  development builds of Linux and Unix, not just with Ubuntu and it's derivatives(developmwent builds).  I also submit and follow upstream bugs of GNU Grub, Kernel.org and Xorg.

"Change" is an interest of mine.  Fixing thinks that are broke 'is' my hobby.

When you "try without installing" from the LiveCD, you would not be running off any "additional drivers," as they would not be installed yet. For PV's with NVidia cards, follow post 3 (liveCD boot options) and use a "nomodeset" boot option.  This is a kernel boot option that will allow {B's with Nvidia cards to work until you can get Ubunut installed and then the correct video drivers installed.


> **johnthei said:**
> well that was a waste of 10 minutes. still get the unreadable graphics when i put the cd in. also at work today i made a new cd just in case there was some problem with it.

all i get when i say unreadable graphics on the Try Ubuntu, is what is my desktop, but it is very useless. I can see where i have a few things on my desktop, i can somewhat see the top tool bar just cos I know it is there, but he size and format of what i am seeing makes it look like the screen is on a very bad lsd trip, and i cannot do anything with it, clicking on something seems to work as a very unviewable screen will show up, and i cannot read what it says.
P'm assuming this is still without using a "nomodeset" option? 

So I guess we'll take this in small steps--  
You're booting off the LiveCD?
If you use a "nomodeset" as a boot option, how does it display?

Let me ask a few questions before I answer anything.  Need to have an idea of what is going on.

1. What PC is it, with what vidoe card and what monitor?
2. You upgraded from what version to what version?
3. Is the OS version 32bit or 64bit?
3. What does it do or not do?
4, What did you try?
5. What video driver are you using?
6, What did you deongrade your kernel to?
7. What did you upgrade your kernel too?
8.  Can you boot into a linux text console?

After answering those questions, we'll go on...  I have an idea on what it is, just by where it's erring out and the workaround, but I need to know the above answers to answer you.

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-06-18
> **zajc said:**
> @MAFoElffen: Thank you for the answer. Honestly I was not aware of such hybrid-graphics cards. I tried to follow your instructions. Unfortunately HP Pavilion g7 BIOS does not have an option to switch switch-able video graphics. I was thinking to upgrade BIOS to see if  they give support to switch cards but I couldn't find any "whatsnew" information. Anyway I would need Windows 7 :shock: to do the upgrade.

I was thinking to start with Ubuntu 10.10 Live CD and then using acpi_call module to switch on/off discrete graphics card ([http://linux-hybrid-graphics.blogspot.com/2010/07/using-acpicall-module-to-switch-onoff.html](http://linux-hybrid-graphics.blogspot.com/2010/07/using-acpicall-module-to-switch-onoff.html)). I think when I forcibly switch off notebook one of the card stays off or on.

Anyway I will wait for another instructions. At least I know what the problem is.
Renaming the xorg.cong to anything else (as you said you already have_ and using a "nomodeset" kernel boot switch on startup should let you boot again  If not, boot up on a 11.04 LiveCD and follow the instructions on the second half of post 3 (right before the point of installing the driver lidtrf in that example) to mount your disk and system...

Once you "can" get to any kind of a terminal or text console, doing
```

lspci -vv | grep VGA

``` will return results that should confirm what it's spec's say about your laptop... It's probably going to say Intel... ATI.

Here is the instructions for doing swiching through the kernel through ACPI calls... written as it relates to Ubuntu:
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/HybridGraphics](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/HybridGraphics)

You have to be able to boot into a text console... but that would get you close enough to install a current catalyst driver... which would get then get you closer to having somewhat acceptable graphics again.

Edit- You're going to have to switch off the ATI card via that APCI call previous to installing the driver.

### Post by zajc on 2011-06-19
I was not able to get into text console. I did the following steps:
- I installed Xubuntu 10.10 64-bit and did all the (257) upgrades ;)
- I installed ATI Catalyst 11.6... I can see GPU switcher...

Everything was working fine. Happy user, again.

Then I did upgrade to Xubuntu 11.04. Blank screen again. In grub2 menu I choose Previous Linux Version, 2.6.35-28 generic recovery mode. At least I can get into text console of failsafeX session. Hurray.

I did grub2 downgrade to 1.98 to see if this helps. Nothing, blank screen agin. At least in kernel 2.6.35-38 generic recovery mode I can get into text console. I renamed xorg.conf into xorg.conf.old. Blank screen.

```
lspci -v | grep VGA
VGA compatible controller: Ati technologies Inc M880G [Mobility Radeon HD 4200]
VGA compatible controller: Ati technologies Inc NI Seymour [AMD Radeon HD 6470]

```

```
grep -i switcheroo /boot/config-2.6.*
/boot/config-2.6.35-28-generic:CONFIG-VGA_SWITCHEROO=y
/boot/config-2.6.38-8-generic:CONFIG-VGA_SWITCHEROO=y

```

I also exeprimented with kernel 2.6.39-0.

With kernel 2.6.35-28 and 2.6.39-0 I can get into failsafeX or normal boot with vesa driver in xorg.conf. But fan is spinning like crazy.
```
Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Configured Video Device"
	Driver		"vesa"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Monitor		"Configured Monitor"
	Device		"Configured Video Device"
EndSection
```

With 2.6.38-8 it is mission impossible. When booting from 2.6.38-8 Xorg.0.log is not even created to see which driver is trying to use.

I attached xorg logs when booting from 2.6.39-0 and xorg.conf is renamed to xorg.conf.old. I never get GUI, at least xorg.log is created. I picked up logs with failsafeX session.

Acpi_call suggested inhttps://help.ubuntu.com/community/HybridGraphics didn't work.

I will probably install Xubuntu 10.10, then upgrade xfce from 4.6 to 4.8 and I will wait for 11.10. I really need notebook ;)

For the "fan spinning like crazy" problem, add 
```

osi_acpi=linux

``` in the kernel boot line ( /etc/default/grub, in line 
```
[FONT=monospace]
[/FONT]GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash osi_acpi-linux "

```This what something I was not expecting:
```
lspci -v | grep VGA
VGA compatible controller: Ati technologies Inc M880G [Mobility Radeon HD 4200]
VGA compatible controller: Ati technologies Inc NI Seymour [AMD Radeon HD 6470]

```What I've seen before hads been Switcxhed graphics of Intel/NVidia or ATI,  Then less common has been Sandy Bridge/Ncidia or ATI... This is the first time I've seen switched graphics of ATI/ATI.  I'd say that ATI's own driver should cover it, expct below where is says ```
[COLOR=Red]Invalid ATI BIOS from int10, the adapter is not VGA-enabled
Invalid video BIOS signature!
GetBIOSParameter failed[/COLOR]

```In this snippet of your xorg.5.log... early on xorf says it sees an ATI Radeon chioset compatible to the xorg opensource driver (radeon) and the vesa driver.  It says it loads the proprietary ATI driver successfully and sees a compatible ATI chipset, but that the video BIOS is not an ATI comaptible BIOS and when it starts making ATI BIOS calls to it, it fails and crashes.

```I'm not sure a rebuild of the ATI Driver again on another kernel is going to get you anywhere.  You might post that xorg.5.log over at the ATI boards.  

The next thing is that you do you a switchable enabled kernel, but again, the video Bios, for some reason will not let you switch the cards via ACPI calls right?

I'm thinking maybe using the xorg opensource radeon, vesa or nouveou drivers as a workaround until there is a switchable solution available to you.... Let me get back to you on that.  (I have to train 3 horses this morning...)

> **zajc said:**
> To continue:
I tried Xubuntu 11.10 (Oneiric Ocelot) Daily Build 19-Jun-2011 64-bit Live USB without success :sad: Blank screen. At the moment the only working distribution is 10.10 ](*,)
What video drivers are loading in 10.10?  Lets see an xorg.0.log from that?  Maybe we can trick the kernel and xorg to use the same environment settings of that version...

> Do I think installing fresh on a recent copy of the LiveCD's ISO is a good idea? ** Yes I do!!!**   You now an idea on what works on your hardware and what doesn't, along  with some skill and knowledge of what to do and how to get there. .   Things have changed alot on your own PC since your first post and on the  recent install ISO.  A clean start would be good.

Please keep me informed on how it goes.First the bad news: You will be disappointed that I have not yet upgraded my video drivers, and my Win7 computer crashed in 10.10, leaving Win7 @ Unable to Start @;...( the reason for the USA keyboard is part of the good news, I am typing this from 11.04 from a brand new LiveCD.)...and me trapped in a Recovery Console I had set as the default boot mode., unable for several hours to find a way to break out, including booting from this DVD.:guitar:

The good news is  first, that I have quit the seemingly endless task of sorting out the remnant evidence of whatever caused my myriad problems, and I have cleared away the debris in such a way that - the good Lord forbid - I could go back to it were it essential. Second, Win7 Fixit was able to rebuild whatever damage had been done to the start sequences - presumably the MBR - without affecting grub and its installation.
Thirdly, I downloaded the Iso files for 11.04 & 10.04 and made DVDs from, them  ( 10.10 is apparently no longer available from Canonical.) and ran 11.04 on my Vista machine: first with aspci=off and nomodset as a precaution, then without any addition to the settngs in grub - incidentally the grub file is much changed , as you probably know. This is mine from the DVD```
:# If you change this file, run 'update-grub' afterwards to update
# /boot/grub/grub.cfg.
# For full documentation of the options in this file, see:
#   info -f grub -n 'Simple configuration'

GRUB_DEFAULT=0
GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT=0
GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT_QUIET=true
GRUB_TIMEOUT=10
GRUB_DISTRIBUTOR=`lsb_release -i -s 2> /dev/null || echo Debian`
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash"
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=""

# Uncomment to enable BadRAM filtering, modify to suit your needs
# This works with Linux (no patch required) and with any kernel that obtains
# the memory map information from GRUB (GNU Mach, kernel of FreeBSD ...)
#GRUB_BADRAM="0x01234567,0xfefefefe,0x89abcdef,0xefefefef"

# Uncomment to disable graphical terminal (grub-pc only)
#GRUB_TERMINAL=console

# The resolution used on graphical terminal
# note that you can use only modes which your graphic card supports via VBE
# you can see them in real GRUB with the command `vbeinfo'
#GRUB_GFXMODE=640x480

# Uncomment if you don't want GRUB to pass "root=UUID=xxx" parameter to Linux
#GRUB_DISABLE_LINUX_UUID=true

# Uncomment to disable generation of recovery mode menu entries
#GRUB_DISABLE_RECOVERY="true"

# Uncomment to get a beep at grub start
#GRUB_INIT_TUNE="480 440 1"
```**End File**
I was  going to write that the present driver was coping quite well; except for some problems in picking up the scroll bar button, but when I called up gedit to bring forward grub to copy it, it opened its window behind the Ubuntu Forum page, and would not come to the front when I clicked on it.So it is not perfect, but good enough to give me a real good feeling of satisfaction.
**Edit:** See note below. **bogan**
The main things I have noticed, and I have not spent all that much time with it, are :  It is a much more convincing arrangement, though It lacks the very professional Launch Pad/Dock it had when I first saw an example, and has gone back to the accustomed three main Menu titles at top-left, as we have long been used to, it has also gone back to listing files in the bottom row when they are minimized.
**Edit: I was mis-lead.**
.....the quote of what I wrote, above, should be almost the direct reverse: " It is a much more convincing arrangement, especially the very professional Launch Pad/Dock I saw in my first glimpse of Unity, - but have missed since due to the confusion - with a special Launcher in the left-hand Dock to access the accustomed three main Menu titles; instead of at top-left, as we have long been used to. Yet more confusingly, that Launcher is labeled " Main Menu" and also shows the same a note as the actual Main Menu Editor does: "Edit Menus" in-stead of "Access Menus". It also no longer lists open files in the bottom row when active or mimimized: instead they are represented by arrow-heads against the parent Launcher Icons. Only a single Active task or Window is listed in the top row, on the left, next to the Apps Button: the Menu Lists which are displayed in the top line are specific to the listed App; the Windows themselves no longer having their Menus listed integrally.   ... **Sorry for the confusion.   bogan    End Edit.**
I went back to the Win7 machine, because I got an e-mail reminding me that my Virus Checker was 38 days old, which meant that the Ubuntu 10.10 installation was the same age also. Considering that the last time I used that machine I knew nothing at all about Unity, I am surprised how familiar it  felt.
So now I cannot put it off any longer and tomorrow, I will work through the accumulated changes, after trying them out on the existing installation.
CHAO !! ** bogan**.             ):P

I seemed to have missed something:  
You said you were running 32bit.  You said that you had an HP Pavillion A320N Desktop, which has an Nvidia  integrated graphics chipset, ASUS A7N8X-LA motherboard...  You also said that you dualboot with Windows.

You said it "is" older legacy Nvidia hardware on older HP hardware (Even though you really never mentioned which nvidia chipset that "sudo hwinfo --framebuffer" says it see's it as). And the current nvidia driver (96.43.19) that you said was for - it doesn't recognised it(?)  And you said Ubuntu has problems recognizing it.
-------------------------------------------------
I had and idea on that, which raised some other questions...  Have you checked the HP support site, Asustek support and/or the NVidia sites for Bios and Firmware upgrades?  When I look at your specs, it says Nvidia NForce 2 and GeoForce 4 MX(?)  My idea is that maybe a Bios/Firmware upfaste might help with that.  I know with 2 of my boxes here that have nForce4 chipset's on ASUS motherboards, that I upgraded the firnware for them- and since then, they have had no problems... and they are "newer" chipsets (nforce4) than your's (nForce2) ...

Updates for your motherboard From [Asustek support]("http://support.asus.com/Download.aspx?SLanguage=en&m=A7N8X&p=1&s=10"):
## If you go to asustek support , one option there is for their 
## site to autorecognise your hardware- good option for you.
 - BIOS version 1010
 - Awdflash BIOS update tool V8.24B (utility needed to install ASUS firmware and BIOS updates)
 - NForce2 all-in-one driver for Linux.
There's a bunch of drivers there for the versions of Windows also.

The firnware updates "there" should help OS'es (like Linux) and other drivers (Like nVidia) to be able to query, recognize and work with your hardware.  Just a thought...

Still working on that modified LiveCD.  Had to move it to another box.

### Post by bogan on 2011-06-21
FAO **MAFolffen**.  HI !!
1. No. I have not yet installed the 270 nVidia driver; but soon will. Getting nearer.
2. Writing of of what I believed to be The Latest Unity Screen, I Posted:
> So it is not perfect, but good enough to give me a real good feeling of satisfaction.
The main things I have noticed, and I have not spent all that much time with it, are : It is a much more convincing arrangement, though It lacks the very professional Launch Pad/Dock it had when I first saw an example, and has gone back to the accustomed three main Menu titles at top-left, as we have long been used to, it has also gone back to listing files in the bottom row when they are minimized.**I was completely wrong,** mis-informed, confused, and not a little annoyed; more so now that I have discovered the main source of my confusion - You asked>  " What is my main complaint ? " - right now it is the lack of a way to tell which Screen Mode I am in, or likely to boot to, or restart into, or to know if it is possible to switch on the trot, without re-booting.
I discovered that the Pop-up menu in the Log-in screen is one of the main things responsible: it over-rides what is set as Default ion the Lock-Screen App.
Hence, if 'Ubuntu' - which I assume to be the 'Unity' Screen - Is It ? - is selected as Default, and a restart done to change from a 'Ubuntu Classic' Screen, the Pop-Up will still show what was last chosen, and if not altered, will take precedence over the Default selection. !!
These Pop-Up options are only visible after the requested UserName has been entered, and even then in only a very faint type. I am not at all surprised  I did not see it,  whilst looking many times, after you had told me it was there.
 So the quote of what I wrote, above, should be almost the direct reverse: " It is a much more convincing arrangement, especially the very professional Launch Pad/Dock I saw in my first glimpse of Unity, - but have missed since due to the confusion - with a special Launcher in the left-hand Dock to access the accustomed three main Menu titles; instead of at top-left, as we have long been used to. Yet more confusingly, that Launcher is labeled " Main Menu" and also shows the same  note as the actual Main Menu Editor does: "Edit Menus" in-stead of "Access Menus". It also no longer lists open files in the bottom row when active or minimized,: instead they are represented by arrow-heads against the parent Launcher Icons. Only a single Active task or Window is listed in the top row, on the left, next to the Apps Button: the Menu Lists which are displayed in the top line are specific to the listed App; the Windows themselves no longer having their Menus listed integrally.
( No doubt you already are aware of all this, but I thought others might be as confused by what I Posted, as I was when I wrote it, I will Edit the original accordingly. Later: I have done so. )
3. You asked anyone in doubt over your Post #280 instructions to send their queries in, so here is one: When You wrote: "From any Text Console..." {... enter the following code. ) Does "any" include a Recovery Console, or a Terminal called from a full graphics screen, or does "Text" exclude either. ? I tried the first ( which needs an active Mouse cursor to activate it, so is presumably a graphics console?? ) as an experiment, and ```
sudo service gdm start
``` gave a very complex refusal about members messages, as there were no menus and Crtl+C did not work, I cant reproduce it here, easily. However, entering 'exit' dropped me out to a log-in box where I could re-enter Ubuntu - now that I know how.
4. Cross-Fingers, up to now, this morning, I have had no problems what so ever, even the WiFi connected to both Wlan adapters immediately!! and Suspend quit to a Stripey screen for a few seconds and then switched to a Screen Saver, before sleeping; and recovered OK. - in fact quite quickly.

                                                                                                                                                :lolflag:


CHAO !!   bogan.      :lolflag:):P

### Post by zajc on 2011-06-21
> **zajc said:**
> I installed Xubuntu 11.04 with Alternate Install CD on **HP Pavilion G7-1007EM** notebook. Everything worked well until yesterday I forcibly powered off the machine. After that I can not get into X session or get into text terminal. The screen is black, keyboard is not working and the fan is spinning like crazy. I tried all the switches in the grub startup you proposed and nothing works. I also renamed xorg.conf to xorg.old (with help of Ubuntu 10.10 Live CD). I made Xubuntu 11.04 Desktop Live CD and it also doesn't work. Black screen, no mater which switches were used. I create Ubuntu 10.10 Desktop Live CD and it works.

I found the **workaround**. It is so simple, that I'm ashamed I never thought about it. Instead 64-bit Xubuntu 11.04 I installed 32-bit Xubuntu 11.04. It looks like it is a bug in 64-bit version which prevent hybrid graphics card to be properly recognized. I successfully installed ATI catalyst 11.6 which recognize Mobility Radeon HD 4200 GPU. I really don't need second GPU, I'm satisfied with 2D graphics (old school).
```
lspci -v | grep VGA
01:05.0 VGA compatible controller: ATI Technologies Inc M880G [Mobility Radeon HD 4200] (prog-if 00 [VGA controller])
02:00.0 VGA compatible controller: ATI Technologies Inc NI Seymour [AMD Radeon HD 6470M] (rev ff) (prog-if ff)
```
Anyway, I will investigate further what will happen with future Xubuntu 64-bit versions and I will report if it will not work ;) I don't know where should I fill the bug.

Now I have another investigation, to find out why SD-MMC card reader is not working :(

Yes, I have installed the 270 nvidia driver, all went well except I had a bother trying to CD to Downloads from a root prompt. Result ? It boots without problems, more or less regardless of what CMDLine additions are made. So far, I have not noticed much difference, there is still occasional slippage when trying to drag a Window or the Scroll button;, hwinfo framebuffer, monitor and vbeinfo,  look much the same - still no info on the VGA driver. Suspend is just the same as before; it sleeps and awakens via the Screen Saver, but requires a press on the 'Enter' key, to get it to respond; it then comes back, momentarily, to the same Stripey Screen it crashed to before, but then comes back to the Screen Saver, from which a Mouse Click will bring it back fully. The intervention of the Screen Saver disguises the fact that recovery is very slow..
I am going to use it a bit in its present state and see whether there is any point in doing a fresh re-install of Ubuntu. It is clear to me now, that a lot of what I thought at the time, were faults or crashes, were due to a lack of basic info on how Unity was supposed to work - it never occurred to me to look in the top line for the menu bars of individual windows and that cost me the loss of a lot of data..

Yes, even though asus made that board, I'm now thinking that the "LA" they may have signed over to HP- meaning HP had their own rights to it and it's BIOS (making it proprietary to them).  As long as HP says it's up-to-date, then you should be okay.

Nvidia's auto-recognize may not work for you then either.  I think it uses the same flash and x controls and I'm not sure they have it set up for their older legacy equipment.  It was worth a try.

The "Good News" is that I finished the modified LiveCD for you.  I had to redo it about 4 times to get the compression right for it to fit on a CD.  It has "nomodeset" set in the CD kernel boot options.  I couldn't pre-install your nvidia driver without going over-sized for a CD, but it should boot into a mode where you can boot and have some kind of graphics.!  

I guess I could have put it on a DVD ISO, but since you had older equipment, I didn't know if you had a DVD drive to be able to do that.  I also thought about adding the install of the driver to the install scripts, but that pushed me over-sized also.  I guess that gives me something to play with laterz.  You will still have to install your driver, but should be able to "boot successfully" now to do that.  I tested it and it seems it's all good.

It's currently uploading to my 4Shared (shared-web storage) account.  When it completes. I'll post the link at the end of this post.

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-06-25
> **johnthei said:**
> ok. d/l and burn iso. put it in drive. reboot. nothing. it does not autorun.  get boot screen with other ubuntu choices as usual.    take it out, try other disc, 11.04 I have it will autorun with the bad graphics just to check boot sequence, it boots to that cd what I cant use...

Seems to boot fine in VirtuaBox... I rechecked the autorun.inf in the ISO.  It seems fine.  Burnt to a CD.  Boots fine on 2 boxes here.  You might try burning another CD at the lowest speed your CD drive supports.

I did the mod's on the "TRY" and "INSTALL" kernel boot lines of the startup menu.  Translation- I hardcode added "nomodeset" to the kernel boot lines of those two menu items.  That way, when you boot it, it already has the right boot option added for someone (you) running an nvidia video chipset.  You said you couldn't get anywhere on the LiveCD to be able to add it yourself... Right?

EDIT-- autorun?  Wait a minute.  Your trying to run WUBI?  I didn't mod this for WUBI.  I don't use it, so I am probably not not one to ask support for it.

That will get you to be able to run the LiveCD and be able to install.  Installing will then install Grub2, which then you can temporarily add nomodeset to your startup, to then be able to install your video drivers.

EDIT-- autorun is for WUBI... I diin't change WUBI.  I don't use it, so really not one to support it.  Is that what you are using?

EDIT-- autorun?  Wait a minute.  Your trying to run WUBI?  I didn't mod this for WUBI.  I don't use it, so I am probably not not one to ask support for it.

That will get you to be able to run the LiveCD and be able to install.  Installing will then install Grub2, which then you can temporarily add nomodeset to your startup, to then be able to install your video drivers.

EDIT-- autoruni is for WUBI... I diin't change WUBI.  I don't use it, so really not one to support it.  Is that what you are using?ok i put it in the drive. it asks what program to open it with....
archive manager, archive mounter, brasero burner, or other. i did get an autorun prompt but it said it wont run in that.
No wonders you were confused by everyone's instructions and not being able to do anything else...  It's really much simpler, once we give you a short explaination and a few pre-instructions.

Okay...  LiveCD's are boot CD's which an Operating System will run from.  Your PC needs to boot off the LiveCD as a medium, instead of your hard disk.  Reboot your PC > On boot, go into your BIOS Settings Menu and insure that the Boot setting is set for your CD/DVD drive before booting to your hard drive (not needed evrytime, but at some point it can be set and left as this) > Save and Exit... Reboot with the LiveCD in your drive....   Your PC should then boot off the CD in your your drive.

This would be the same if you were trying to install any operating system (Windows, Linux, Unix, MAC OSX, etc.  Yes, not all those are on LiveCD's, but you boot off their Installt CD to be in an OS (or in-between OS) to be able to install that target OS.

Once you boot off that LiveCD, then.... All those instruction we gave you before... that were confusing because they didn't work for you, should starrt making sense and start working as described previously.

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-06-29
> **SeanCly10 said:**
> Hopefully someone can help me out here; I've been pulling my hair out on this one with no luck so far.

My system is running a [COLOR=Red]GeForce 6800, with the 173 driver[/COLOR]. Cannot boot into normal mode; I get a momentary splash screen which flickers a few times, then the monitor goes black, then it turns off about 15 seconds later. I can boot into recovery mode and use failsafe graphics, but that's it.

Can someone smarter than me offer insight?
My main box has 2 XFX GeoForce 6800 Ultra's bridged SLI... so I have some intimate experience which these cards.  

Please post your /var/log/xorg.2.log.  I would usually say xorg.o.log- but what it does is goes from 0 to 5 (keeps 5 logs back) and the log I need to see is whichever one has where it's trying to load your nvidia drivers.  I don't need to see any log where it tried to run in recover, low-graphics or safe mode, because those logs will be while it is using a fallback generic driver instead of the nvidia drivers...

Also post the results of 
```

lspci -v | grep VGA

```I think what we might find is that  the nvidia drivers are erroring out... Why do I suspect this?  Well, your comment on the driver you are using... 
> 
[COLOR=Red][COLOR=Black]GeForce 6800[/COLOR], with the 173 driver
[/COLOR][COLOR=Red]
[/COLOR]and what Linux is reporting
```

Vendor: "NVIDIA Corporation"
Device: "nv40 Board - p212-0  "

``` Should be using nvidia-current from the additional drivers applet or the nvidia driver version 275.09.07, which was released 2011.06.14... Geoforce 6000 series cards and up use the 270 series drivers.  The 173 series drivers were for older nividia cards.

Once you post your xorg.n.log so we can verify that as your problem, then I'll refer you to one of my posts with instructions to uninstall the wrong drivers and correct that.

---

Just another question-
1. If you boot up in safe, rescue, failsafe, low-graphics mode... > go to Additional Drivers (also called jockey or jockey-gtk) > What driver does it say you are using?  You said 173, your xorg.conf said nvidia... The xoeg.2.log says you're using [COLOR=Blue]NOUVEAU driver for NVIDIA[/COLOR] but the driver date was 2011.01.07 (I know there is newer for this driver)... and it can't find your video card... Although the PCI bus finds your card and reports it to Linux correctly.

I'm thinking that what you "might" see in Jockey / Additional Drivers is a driver called "Experimental 3D Driver for NVidia"... which is not as good as the proprietary nvidia driver's yet, but getiing close for the other / older cards...  But something is still definitely wrong there!!!  

One it doesn't see your card, so it isn't working. I used this driver while testing natty prereleases... and know there was lots of changes in March through May...  I'm still using this driver for 2 of my Oneiric test boxes (nv10 & nv20)... But for the 6800-- use the prioprietary. (Instructions post 280)...

Go to this post and follow these instructions:  
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10909540&postcount=280](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10909540&postcount=280)

Download/Use the pertinent driver from nvidia, which for you would be the 270 series.  Following those instructions will help you to remove any previous drivers and install the current driver, and try to prevent any conflicts... along with a few workarounds that fixed some recent problems.

Now for the install issue.  A bit beyond the scope of this Graphics Issues Thread.  For the past day I've been quandering what to do about that.

The correct thing to do is to tell you to start another thread in "Installations and Upgrades"
titled something like "Freezes During Installation From LiveCD" and include the description you gave from post 381 ([http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10992504&postcount=381](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10992504&postcount=381))

The important part in that description is to say you can run gnome from a modified version of the Natty 11.04 LiveCD with no problems, but if you try to install, it freezes during the install process...

Then reference / describe everything about that Install Screen (take a picture with your phone or whatever to post), etc.

You would need to post a few files to be able to trace down where it's locking up.  To do that- Boot from the LiveCD > Try > Let is boot to the Live filesystem > Start Install to where it freezes > Press cancel and get back to the Live Filesystem...  Then...

/var/log/sys
/var/log/dmesg

```

It's erring from an install process, in a step  where it's checking and trying to use lots of different devices.  I'm real good with graphics, but there is others (some of them friends) out there in the Install Forum who I know are better than I at tracing that kind of error down.

Once you get that thread going, pop back here and post a link to it.  I'll try to follow it to help you provide background and help you with understandable "translations."

In the meanwhile, I'll debug that LiveCD Image in Virtual and see what system devices it is trying to hit in the screen you described.  You said the very first Install screen past the languages and keyboards?

> **SeanCly10 said:**

```

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-06-30
> **SeanCly10 said:**
> Right, 280, sorry about that. Typed that at about 2:30 am. :P

One thing about the code examples in solution one, though...this line:

```
sudo apt-get install linux-headers-'uname -r'

```gave me an error when I ran it. I thought it wasn't a big deal when it did since I remembered already downloading the headers for another fix I tried earlier. This line is correct, yes?
if you type  
```
uname -r
```into a terminal, it should return whatever the current kernel version is.... So the above command 
```
sudo apt-get install linux-headers-'uname -r'

```Should be saying something similar to 
```
sudo apt-get install linux-headers-2.6.38-8-generic

```which should install the linux header files of the current kernel... If you get an error, try it in longhand... Because --> A boot hanging at the message "Checking Battery State"- 9 out of 10 times is usually the linux header file being missing or corrupt.  ...And if that file "was" also missing when you installed your video driver... Troubles.

Even bigger troubles is multiple versions of Video drivers loaded at the same time, which you said yo had going on. Which:
```
sudo apt-get remove --purge nvidia-*
```should have taken care of... as a prep to the install.

---

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-01
> **SeanCly10 said:**
> Okay, started over with post 280, going through each line one at the time. All went well up until I ran the installer for the Nvidia driver, and it said it was 'unable to load nvidia.ko' and could not continue.

At that point I just said to heck with it and started over completely, wiping the install and reinstalling it fresh. From there, I tried to do post 280 over again, both solutions I and II, neither to any effect. I tried what I had before, booting into recovery mode and enabling the driver in jockey, and on the next boot attempt I got boot text again. 

This time, it made it well past the 'Checking battery state' message, and now hangs on 'Stopping save udev log and update rules', with [OK] over to the right. Also of note, an earlier line reads 'Starting load fallback graphics devices', with [fail] on the right.

Oh, and jockey in failsafe mode lists the nvidia_173 driver as activated but not in use.

So that's where I am now. As before, here are the particulars:

Notice the driver version highlighted above in blue... and that the error at the end is that the driver cannot identify your GPU.

These are the GPU's the 173 driver supports:
> Below are the legacy GPUs that are no longer supported in the unified driver. 
These GPUs will continue to be maintained through the special legacy NVIDIA 
GPU driver releases. 
 
The 173.14.xx driver supports the following set of GPUs: 
 
 
    NVIDIA GPU product                    Device         PCI ID 
    ----------------------------------    ----------------------------------

    Quadro FX 1100                                                             0x034E        Notice that the Geoforce 6800 is not one of those supported cards for that driver...  Look in your log where I highlighted in blue... See the version number?  NVidia driver "173"-- is not for Geoforce 6800 card.  In fact, you should have it listsed in your blacklist file so it doesn't accidentallay install.  You seem to have that installed again and that is why your pc does not see your GPU.

Notice the version numbers in  this Extract of MY xorg.0.log:

```Remember that my log is with the same Video card as yours, running on the same kernel...

Now Look at this list of GPU's supported by the 270 series driver (also aka as the nvidia-current driver in Ubuntu):
>  
________________________________________
Appendix A. Supported NVIDIA GPU Products 
________________________________________
For the most complete and accurate listing of supported GPUs, please see the 
Supported Products List, available from the NVIDIA Linux x86_64 Graphics 
Driver download page. Please go to [http://www.nvidia.com/object/unix.html](http://www.nvidia.com/object/unix.html), 
follow the Archive link under the Linux x86_64 heading, follow the link for 
the 270.41.06 driver, and then go to the Supported Products List. 
 
For an explanation of the VDPAU features column, please refer to the section 
called "VdpDecoder" of Appendix H. 
 
Note that the list of supported GPU products provided below and on the driver 
download page is provided to indicate which GPUs are supported by a particular 
driver version. Some designs incorporating supported GPUs may not be 
compatible with the NVIDIA Linux driver: in particular, notebook and 
all-in-one desktop designs with switchable (hybrid) or Optimus graphics will 
not work if means to disable the integrated graphics in hardware are not 
available. Hardware designs will vary from manufacturer to manufacturer, so 
please consult with a system's manufacturer to determine whether that 
particular system is compatible.

Notice that the Geoforce 6800 is at the top of that list?  Remember that each post of yours that you mrntioned the 173 series drivers, that I tried to correct you that that is the wrong driver for your card and that you should be using the 270 series driver or the ubuntu nvidia-current driver...

So to explain this as clear as possible-  The nvidia 173 series does not work with the Nvidia Geoforce 6800 video card (yours).  *Stop trying to use it*. Your card needs to use the Nvidia 270 series...

If you followed the instructions in post 280... and it still ended up as the 173, then I can conclude that you manually downloaded the wrong driver.  After doing that, you installed that incorrect driver.

I'd hate to say this, but please download the *correct driver* and start over again.

---

### Post by SeanCly10 on 2011-07-01
To make me not look like a complete idiot...

The link provided goes to Nvidia's website, where they give the driver I downloaded (275, I believe) as the one for the Geforce 6 series. In addition, when you look at the drivers available in jockey, it lists the nvidia-173 as being the one for 'Geforce 5 series and Geforce 6 series'.

I see that those notes were incorrect, so now I'll go looking for the correct driver. Thank you for continuing to keep me on point, despite my apparent hardheadedness. :oops:

---

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-01
> **SeanCly10 said:**
> To make me not look like a complete idiot...

The link provided goes to Nvidia's website, where they give the driver I downloaded (275, I believe) as the one for the Geforce 6 series. In addition, when you look at the drivers available in jockey, it lists the nvidia-173 as being the one for 'Geforce 5 series and Geforce 6 series'.

I see that those notes were incorrect, so now I'll go looking for the correct driver. Thank you for continuing to keep me on point, despite my apparent hardheadedness. :oops:
LOL-- I have somewhat of a twisted sense of humor.

No problem.  I tried not to sound contrascending.  I know getting the right driver should really help with this.

There is somewhat of a gray area in different distributions that has caused some confusion in the Geoforce 6000 to 6200 cards... with their own "opensource drivers," but the Goeforce 6800 is in the Current series drivers for all of those.  Then there is the version numbers of the distr's Graphics dirvers-- For instance natty's--

nvidia-current is the 270 series nvidia driver (current is 275.-9.07), but ubuntu has the package numbered as 270-41-06-0ubuntu1. 

nvidia-173 is the 173 series nvidia driver (current is 173.14.30), but ubuntu has the package numbered as 173.14.30-0ubuntu1. 

nvidia-96 is the 96 series nvidia driver (current is 96.43.19), but ubuntu has the package numbered as 96.43.19-0ubuntu8.

The quotes I used in the previous post where it ID'ed which cards used which drivers, is from Nvidia's Driver ReadMe File, Appendix A, Supported Hardware.

**If** you downloaded [NVidia"s 275.-9.07 driver]("http://www.nvidia.com/object/linux-display-ia32-275.09.07-driver.html") and it is coming up installing as a 173 series for some reason, then the problem is not you...  But one thing you want to check again is the blacklist file and insure that the nvdia-173 is blacklisted in it.

Congrats on getting past your graphics display troubles.

The correct thing to do is to tell you to start another (a separate thread of your own) thread in "Installations and Upgrades" titled something like "Freezes During Installation From LiveCD" and include the description you gave from post 381 ([http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10992504&postcount=381](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10992504&postcount=381))

The important part in that description is to say you can run gnome from a  modified version of the Natty 11.04 LiveCD with no problems, but if you  try to install, it freezes during the install process... Then describe everything about that Install Screen (take a picture with your phone or whatever to post), etc.

You would need to post a few files to be able to trace down where it's  locking up.  To do that- Boot from the LiveCD > Try > Let it boot  to the Desktop > Select the Install Icon and let it install to where it freezes > Press  cancel and get back to the Desktop...  

What you will be doing is trying to recreate your error and get to where the Install process stops for you.  Once it gets there, you need to get back to the desktop of the LiveCD or to a Console prompt to be able to copy the syslog and dmesg log so that you can post them...

Copy and post these 2 files to that thread:
/var/log/syslog
/var/log/dmesg

and from a terminal. the full results of 

Another reason to do this from a fresh thread= It's past the graphics  issue for npw- at least as for the LiveCD.  It's erring from an install  process, in a step  where it's checking and trying to use lots of  different devices.  I'm real good with graphics, but there is others  (some of them friends) out there in the Install Forum who I know are  better than I at tracing that kind of error down.

Once you get that thread going, pop back here and post a link to it.   I'll try to follow it to help you provide background and help you with  understandable "translations."

In the meanwhile, I'll debug that LiveCD Image in Virtual and see what  system devices it is trying to hit in the screen you described.  You  said the very first Install screen past the languages and keyboards?I edited the instructions above.  I had asked you to start another thread of your own and to post those items to it... But no matters, I'll try to take care of you with those items here, even though they are off-topic and beyond the scope of a graphics issue now.  Your hardware does seem fairly standard, so it will be curious to what the installer is freezing on.

[it locked up at the forward button on the first screen] but now I have 11.04, i managed to do the install additional drivers from writing down where on the screen each word was [looking at another PC with 11.04 on it] and got to  system and admin and then additional drivers and pretty much guessed  what to click on that screen...since it was impossible to read the words..

and choose it and get the log on screen and see barely at the bottom i can pick classic and some other options. I picked classic and here i am.
 
i have some nvidia driver enabled [by lucky guessing of something to try] but it only gives me 1024 x 768 graphics. screens are all a bit off.

 where do you suggest i start now on this..what teminal command to try to figure out something to improve the graphics?Almost there now...

When it is booting from the harddisk... press the shift key intermittantly until the Grub Menu displays... then press the "e" key to put it into an edit mode on the main menu item > arrow down to the kernel boot line, ehich will start with linux... go to the end of that line and add to word **nomodeset**  > Press <cntrl><x> and it will boot that menu item.

When it boots up, go to System > Administration > Aditional Drivers ... Select nvidia-96 as the driver. after it downloads and installs, press the Activate Button.

Tell me how it goes.

Good Job so far... after you get passed the login screen and gnome starts up > Go to System > Administration > Additional Drivers > Select the nvidia-current driver. After it downloads and installs, select the activate button.  That will install the correct nvidia driver for your card.

okay-- boot in rescue mode and rename /etc/X11/xorg.conf to xorg.conf.old... post it here.

Also post your /var/log/xorg.1.log.  It keeps xorg.n.log's from 0 to 5... 1t should be one back, where it was trying to start the graphics with that driver and show where it erred out.

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-02
> **TheGeorgian said:**
> How do I boot in rescue mode? Because all I have is a black screen, the only thing I can think of is to log in using the install cd. Is there an easier way?
if it is the only OS on your PC, press the <shift> key intermittently as it boots > the Grub menu should dislpay when it gets to that point.  At the Grub menu, arrow down one to the second boot item, which should say something like Ubuntu, 2.6.38.8, Rescue... Pick that option.

It will boot to a VGA kind of screen with a box menu, where one option will be boot in safe / low graphics mode, select that option.

if that doesn't work, look at post number 3, boot from a LiveCD.  Mount the hard disk that your install is located, figure out how many attempted boots back you have to go to to get the correct xorg.n.log file that has the driver error in it.

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-02
> **TheGeorgian said:**
> I couldn't get rescue mode to work, so I am using the livecd. Here is xorg.conf:
```

Section "Device"
       Identifier    "Default Device"
       Option    "NoLogo"    "True"
EndSection

[  2047.729]  ddxSigGiveUp: Closing log                      

```
Your xorg.conf is not the one it was using, as that one would be invalid (not enough info) to point it to an Nvidia driver as it shows being loaded in the xorg.0.log... although the xorg.conf info doesn't show up in the xorg.0.log as it should also.

What your log says is that your monitor is not returning the EDID data back to your video card, so that the card cannot figure out what resolutions it is capable of displaying.  Becuase of that, the nvidia driver's nvidia-auto-select mode to be able to work...The driver tried to compensate by setting a mode of 1024x768.

Check post 280 and look for the blacklist file.  Make sure your blacklist file matches.  Then, If using the LiveCD, follow then instructions in post 3, the second half, and mount the sys of your install... Up through the instructions at step 4.  Instead of step 5 in that post, do this
```

sudo nvidia-xconfig

```That will generate a new, clean xorg.conf file.  If rebooting doesn;t comeup right, edit your xorg.conf file, extract which will look similar to"
```

Section "Screen"         
  Identifier "Screen0"         
  Device     "Card0"         
  Monitor    "Monitor0"         
  DefaultDepth 24         
  SubSection "Display"                 
    Viewport  0 0                 
    Depth     24                 
    [COLOR=Red]Modes     "1024x768"[/COLOR]         
  EndSubSection 
EndSection 

Section "Monitor" 
  Identifier      "Monitor1" 
  VendorName      "Bigname" 
  ModelName       "BestModel" 
  [COLOR=Red]Modeline "1024x768 60.00"   63.50  1024 1072 1176 1328  768 771 775 798 -hsync +vsync[/COLOR]
  Option          "DPMS" 
EndSection 

```Since your monitor is not returning data back...  Look at the lines highlighted in red above, you may have to add those lines to the appropriate sections of your xorg.conf file to tell the driver what to use as data, manually.

---

    Identifier     "Device0"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    VendorName     "NVIDIA Corporation"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Device0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1024x768"
    EndSubSection
EndSection                      

```There is still only one log file in var/log and it still has the same number: Xorg.0.log, so i suspect it's the same file i already posted.

Any ideas?
One sidenote- when posting logs, please use the " # " icon of the forum posting bar and paste the text within the CODE tags... It will [ut the text into a text box with scroll bars and make the post shorter and leave it easier to review.

Yes, there is something still going on deeper.  Please post your /var/log/syslog.  Maybe it might show something else going on.  How much memory does this PC have?  What is the monitor brand and model? Does it have a "manufactured date" on it's tag?   What resolutions did this monitor display in the past?

Can you boot from a LiveCD and run this?
```

xrandr -q

```
I'm thinking it may be mis-allocating video memory during it's graphics boot or it may need to generate a separate EDID binary file to use for data for the monitor...

---

### Post by TheGeorgian on 2011-07-03
[SIZE=-1]Hi MAFoElffen.

Thanks again. Since we last spoke I tried messing around with the various options for the line GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX-DEFUALT='quiet splash' in my grub file. I tried adding nomodeset which was suggested in the following post:
[http://ubuntu-tutorials.com/2010/05/06/ubuntu-10-04-lucid-blank-screen-at-startup-workaround/](http://ubuntu-tutorials.com/2010/05/06/ubuntu-10-04-lucid-blank-screen-at-startup-workaround/)_ _but that did not help. My monitor is a LG FLATRON W1934S. It was manufactured Feb 2008.  I can't remember what resolutions it ran in the past :(  I believe I have 8Gb of ram.

P.S. thanks for explaining that trick with pasting text, I was wondering how to do that. By the way, considering that my computer and monitor booted fine before I installed the nvidia driver from system->administration->additional drivers, could the problem be there? I read in other threads that people were having success by downloading the nvidia driver from the nvidia site. I tried that the other day with ubuntu 10.04, but the steps necessary to install it required so many workarounds, and they ended up not working for me. I never succeeded in installing the driver and gave up. That's when I tried a clean install of 11.04 and joined the current thread.

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-03
> **TheGeorgian said:**
> [SIZE=-1]Hi MAFoElffen.

Thanks again. Since we last spoke I tried messing around with the various options for the line GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX-DEFUALT='quiet splash' in my grub file. I tried adding nomodeset which was suggested in the following post:
[http://ubuntu-tutorials.com/2010/05/06/ubuntu-10-04-lucid-blank-screen-at-startup-workaround/](http://ubuntu-tutorials.com/2010/05/06/ubuntu-10-04-lucid-blank-screen-at-startup-workaround/)but that did not help. My monitor is a LG FLATRON W1934S. It was manufactured Feb 2008.  I can't remember what resolutions it ran in the past :(  I believe I have 8Gb of ram.

P.S. thanks for explaining that trick with pasting text, I was wondering how to do that. By the way, considering that my computer and monitor booted fine before I installed the nvidia driver from system->administration->additional drivers, could the problem be there? I read in other threads that people were having success by downloading the nvidia driver from the nvidia site. I tried that the other day with ubuntu 10.04, but the steps necessary to install it required so many workarounds, and they ended up not working for me. I never succeeded in installing the driver and gave up. That's when I tried a clean install of 11.04 and joined the current thread.

In any case,  here is the output from xrandr -q:

```

Screen 0: minimum 320 x 200, current 1024 x 768, maximum 8192 x 8192
DVI-I-1 connected 1024x768+0+0 (normal left inverted right x axis y axis) 0mm x 0mm
  1024x768       60.0*
  800x600        60.3     56.2
  848x480        60.0
  640x480        59.9
VGA-1 connected 1024x768+0+0 (normal left inverted right x axis y axis) 410mm x 256mm
  1440x900       59.9 +   75.0
  1280x1024      75.0     60.0
  1152x864       75.0
  1024x768       75.1     75.0     70.1     60.0*
  832x624        74.6
  800x600        72.2     75.0     60.3     56.2
  640x480        72.8     75.0     66.7     60.0
  720x400        70.1

```My syslog file is so long it wont post. Is there a sub portion I should look for? I have restarted my computer maybe 25 times since the fresh install, and the syslog file appears to hold logs for all of those times.[/SIZE]
On the /etc/default/grub... [SIZE=-1]GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX-DEFUALT= ... Try adding vmalloc=192MB  After editing, save and rerun update-grub.

Do you have 2 monitors connected via VGA-1 and DVI-I-1? Your xorg.conf isn't showing that.  Conflict?  Have you tried disconnecting either and trying?

Look at Post #280 and try that...

I then get an error that nouveau is running. The problem is that I am running off of the live cd and doing everything from a terminal using Applications->Accessories->Terminal. To access files I follow the steps I found from the link [http://www.robertbeal.com/562/rebuilding-grub2-grub-cfg-from-ubuntu-live-cd](http://www.robertbeal.com/562/rebuilding-grub2-grub-cfg-from-ubuntu-live-cd). I am not in a text only environment. There is the usual ubuntu background etc... I tried editing [COLOR=Red][COLOR=Black] /etc/default/grub to add [/COLOR][/COLOR]GRUB_GFXPAYLOAD=text as suggested in post 2, but no matter what, without the live cd, all I ever get is a black screen.
In that case. here is what I want you to do...

1. Print out or copy this post.

2. Print out or copy Post 3.

3. Print out or copy Post 280 (because that is now half-installed.)

4. Boot up on the LiveCD.

5. Follow the intsructions in post 2 to mount the sys of your install.

6. Open a browser window and get back to this post...
 
7. Open a terminal and edit /etc/default/grub... 
```

sudo gedit /etc/default/grub

```While in Gedit, save the existing file as a backup file in your /Home/Documents directory.  

Then copy the below text and replace the exsiting text from the grub file with it. (Has all the changes you want already.  Save it as /etc/default/grub
```

# If you change this file, run 'update-grub' afterwards to update
# /boot/grub/grub.cfg.
# For full documentation of the options in this file, see:
#   info -f grub -n 'Simple configuration'

GRUB_DEFAULT=0
# GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT=0
GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT_QUIET=false
GRUB_TIMEOUT=20
GRUB_DISTRIBUTOR=`lsb_release -i -s 2> /dev/null || echo Debian`
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash vga=0x0318 nomodeset "
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=""

# Uncomment to enable BadRAM filtering, modify to suit your needs
# This works with Linux (no patch required) and with any kernel that obtains
# the memory map information from GRUB (GNU Mach, kernel of FreeBSD ...)
#GRUB_BADRAM="0x01234567,0xfefefefe,0x89abcdef,0xefefefef"

# Uncomment to disable graphical terminal (grub-pc only)
#GRUB_TERMINAL=console

# The resolution used on graphical terminal
# note that you can use only modes which your graphic card supports via VBE
# you can see them in real GRUB with the command `vbeinfo'
GRUB_GFXMODE=800x600x24

# Uncomment if you don't want GRUB to pass "root=UUID=xxx" parameter to Linux
#GRUB_DISABLE_LINUX_UUID=true

# Uncomment to disable generation of recovery mode menu entries
#GRUB_DISABLE_RECOVERY="true"

# Uncomment to get a beep at grub start
#GRUB_INIT_TUNE="480 440 1"

```Copied and pasted this into that file? And saved as /etc/default/grub?  Good leave the file in the editor so the edotor is still open in Sudo mode and continue... 
[/code]
Open this file in the editor: /boot/grub.d/40_custom. Copy the text in the box below over/replacing everything in it and save...
```
#!/bin/sh
exec tail -n +3 $0
# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.
#
##     This adds a custom menu item to the Grub Menu      ##
##     to add an item to start in a Text Console Session      ##
##            Working with Ubuntu 11.04.                             ##
##                                                                                ##
#
menuentry 'Ubuntu Text Console, with Linux 2.6.38-8-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    recordfail
    set gfxpayload=$linux_gfx_mode
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(/dev/sdb,msdos3)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root b42d2b81-9547-4acf-a0e1-e33fd6d4af38
    linux    BOOT_IMAGE=/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-8-generic  root=UUID=b42d2b81-9547-4acf-a0e1-e33fd6d4af38 ro --verbose  text    
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-2.6.38-8-generic
}
##          End of Ubuntu Text Console Entry section          ##

```Save file. Now run this command 
```

sudo update-grub
sudo reboot

```Remove the CD and hit enter.  Will reboot.  When it is booting, press the shift key again and again.  See if the Grub menu comes up.  If it does, arrow down to the last item, which should say " Ubuntu Text Console, with Linux 2.6.38-8-generic"  Select that option.  I modified it with your kernel version and your UUID (from data from your xorg.0.log).  It should boot the Linux kernel, with verbose logging/messages turned on... and end up in a text console mode, where you would have to type in your userid and password.

From there we can try a few things, change and install things... Start up GDM or kill the process if needed without having to reboot all the time, while trying things out...

That is, if we can get to a Grub Menu to select that option!!! Tell me how it goes.

If all goes well on that--> Then you could go back to post #280 and reinstall that driver... cause at the moment, that one is only half installed.

The xorg.0.log was also showning it half installed:

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-03
> **TheGeorgian said:**
> [SIZE=-1]
In any case,  here is the output from xrandr -q:
```

Screen 0: minimum 320 x 200, current 1024 x 768, maximum 8192 x 8192
[COLOR=Red]DVI-I-1[/COLOR] connected 1024x768+0+0 (normal left inverted right x axis y axis) 0mm x 0mm
  1024x768       60.0[COLOR=Red]*[/COLOR]
  800x600        60.3     56.2
  848x480        60.0
  640x480        59.9
[COLOR=Red]VGA-1 [/COLOR]connected 1024x768+0+0 (normal left inverted right x axis y axis) 410mm x 256mm
  1440x900       59.9 [COLOR=Red]+ [/COLOR]  75.0
  1280x1024      75.0     60.0
  1152x864       75.0
  1024x768       75.1     75.0     70.1     60.0*
  832x624        74.6
  800x600        72.2     75.0     60.3     56.2
  640x480        72.8     75.0     66.7     60.0
  720x400        70.1
```[/SIZE]
Okay, so here is what is wrong with this... It says that both ports DVI-I-1 and VGA-1 are connected to montiors... which you say only one display is connected --> VGA-1.

The problem with that is that- well, see the " * " (star) looking character in the results above (under DVI-I-1)?  That is the port and mode that xrandr is reporting being used, which is not really being physically used!!!

The other port VGA-1 is reporting that as the prefered connection and mode (look for the " + " (plus) sign... but is coming up as a black screen

Installing the right drivers using the instructions from post #280 may correct that.  If not we can create an xrandr personal profile file to turn off the output at DVI-I-1 and set the VGA-1 port as output...  

At the end of all that, try to start and then post the xorg.0.log here...

---

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-03
If the previous post didn't help and it's still confused...

Save this in your Home directory named as " .xprofile "
```

xrandr --output DVI-I-1 --off
xrandr --output VGA-1 --mode 1024x768 --rate 60

```Then add this to the existing Monitor Section of your xorg.conf file
```

   # This optional entry specifies the position of the monitor within the X screen. 
   Option        "Position" "1024 0"         

```Then add another Monitor Section for the DVI-I-1
```

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "DVI-I-1"
    VendorName     "None"
    ModelName      "None"
    # This optional entry specifies that the monitor should be ignored         
    # entirely, and not reported through RandR.  This is useful if the
    # hardware reports  the  presence  of  outputs  that do not exist.
    # This is what is going on with your ports.         
    Option "Ignore"  "true" 
 EndSection
```That should try to un-confuse things.

But still, no desktop. The red text above tells me something is still wrong, but I'm not well-versed enough in the messages detailed to know what. Regardless, still black, shut off screen on bootup. More, when booting to failsafe mode, there are no drivers to activate in jockey.

Help? :-(

### Post by johnthei on 2011-07-04
I tried to install the 173 driver from symnatic package. failed. 

will try again and post the log of what it says on the failure notice.

### Post by TheGeorgian on 2011-07-04
Hi MAFoElffen,

I tried your post 423, but it did not work. Unfortunately, I was never able to get the grub menu and my screen remained blank on reboot, there is no video output to the monitor at all. Now here's a question, if I do a fresh install of 11.04, what's the danger in simply never installing the nvidia driver? I don't need great graphics for this computer, and I don't care about the new Unity interface. The main purpose of this computer is to run simulations I code in fortran, and to process results with matlab. At this point, I am thinking of any solution that leaves me with a robust system that consistently boots properly. For that matter, is there any danger in just installing ubuntu 8.10? 

P.S. since originally posting this, I reinstalled 11.04, and it works fine so long as I do not install the nvidia driver. I will try rebooting a bunch of times to test consistency.

Thanks as always

---

To make sure this log file includes as much relevant information as possible, please start the X server from Ubuntu 10.10 with

```

nvidia-bug-report.sh

```after the problem has occurred. Use the <cntrl><alt><F1> hotkeys to get to a text console. 

Please run the bug report script with X running (this will ensure that data points only available when the device is initialized, such as the VBIOS revision, are captured in the log file).

Even if `nvidia-bug-report.sh` appears to hang, it may still have collected enough useful information about your problem. In this case, please provide the partial log file generated, if any.

In case of NVIDIA Linux graphics driver installation problems (when using one of the official .run installers), please attach the NVIDIA installer log file, /var/log/nvidia-installer.log.

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-04
> **TheGeorgian said:**
> Hi MAFoElffen,

I tried your post 423, but it did not work. Unfortunately, I was never able to get the grub menu and my screen remained blank on reboot, there is no video output to the monitor at all. Now here's a question, if I do a fresh install of 11.04, what's the danger in simply never installing the nvidia driver? I don't need great graphics for this computer, and I don't care about the new Unity interface. The main purpose of this computer is to run simulations I code in fortran, and to process results with matlab. At this point, I am thinking of any solution that leaves me with a robust system that consistently boots properly. For that matter, is there any danger in just installing ubuntu 8.10? 

P.S. since originally posting this, I reinstalled 11.04, and it works fine so long as I do not install the nvidia driver. I will try rebooting a bunch of times to test consistency.

Thanks as always
No danger at all... Just add the "nomodeset" switch to the  line "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT" in /etc/default/grub file, save and run update-grub to pick up that change and make it persistent.  That will allow Ubuntu to boot with your nvidia card without having the nvidia drivers installed.

---

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-05
> **SeanCly10 said:**
> Alright, I did the best I could. Regarding the nvidia bug report, once the error occurs, whatever it is, no key command makes any noticeable difference. The only way I can get to a terminal is to either boot in failsafe mode and back out of the x-server to a terminal, or use 'quiet splash text' in the GRUB boot string and never start X at all.

Here's the results that I got.Understood.

One more file to post: ~/.xsession.errors.  Try to start one more time with X, then next time start with a text switch in console. Then do
```

cp ~/.xsession.errors ~/Documents/xsession.errors

```then post

Now, from your xorg.0.log saying it had a GLX file, but then it couldn't find it... Then some of the other logs:
```

nvidia-kernel-common:
    Installed: (none)
    Candidate: 20080825+1ubuntu2   
    Version table:      20080825+1ubuntu2 0
      500 http://us.archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ lucid/restricted Packages[FONT=monospace]

etc.

```
Which says there is no NVidia drivers installed there.

Then the extensions sya there is a partial install of nvidia... with the only file being some kind of link to an Nvidia GLX file... which in your previous error, that file should not have tried to load in failsafe mode, when it was trying to load as Vesa.

Then there is your [/FONT] nvidia-installer.log file that says that there was already Nvidia drivers loaded when the last was installed... Problem nvidia say with that is that it might not install correctly if you don't do an uninstall first.  That might have affected the options when it installed, as they don't seem right for an install.  Then in said it had the incorrect source and library files...

Seems to me-- Go to post #280.  Look at step 1 to uninstall any old instances of the nvidia drivers.  Install the header and library files.  Install the driver again.  Afterwards, repost the nvidia-installer.log file.
[FONT=monospace]
[/FONT]
