### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-08
> **johnthei said:**
> I share this PC with my wife so I dont have all the time to use it. She is getting a bit tired of me having it so she can't use it allot lately.....I do have some free time tonight, I might get a chance to try a total new install again like I planned with original ISO and then try to get the proprietary nvidia set up from guessing again where everything is since the graphics are so hard to read at this point.... Whatever I do I will post it.
Maybe we could work on the whole "picture."

I don't mean to get off-topic, but... After hearing this. I feel compelled to share a little experience on that.  My fiance is not "technical."  Before me, she had a cell phone that she maybe spent less than 50 minutes a month on, thought of as an annoyance, had very little experience on computers at all... hated computers!  Now she texts, takes pictures, shares them, knows her way around her phone, spends hours on my laptop that she has adopted as her own.  She chats with her friends, keeps up on news, plays her local installed games and facebook games... enough that I now refer to her as my gamer chic...

Just as if I was supporting a Corporate CEO, I keep that laptop on "stable code."  I keep her setup as something she knows where everything is and can get to it...  We now share a passion with computers, but in different ways.  She supports my passion of development, testing, hacking operating systems, trying out new stuff and helping people... but that only works if i keep her happy by openly talking (sharing) about what I'm doing, translating in a way she can understand... and ensuring that she has access to what she wants to do, when she wants to do it. 

I know my fiance has no patience at all!!!  If she had a problem and couldn't get to something... that laptop might just be in danger of getting aerial. (Realistically!!!)

I usually have between 5 to 7 PC's setup at one time.  Her adopted Laptop and my main box are setup right next to each other.  That way it's shared time- not detracting from her and what we share together. 

I applaud that you want to get your hands dirty, make things better and learn by doing.  That's how my mind works and how I learn.  Linux is a great platform to do that, cheaply, with little expense.  It also has great community support.

One idea I have that I want to share with you- Put a "wanted add" in your local paper or your work newsletter... When people get a new PC to replace an older PC that they think no longer works for them, that older PC that they are replacing could find a home with you?  You might be surprised that that old PC might be newer or better than the PC you have now!  Then you and your wife could share time, without... You know what I mean?  And that way it also would not be taking from both your pocket books and causing other problems.

Something to think about and talk with her about?  I think passions should be "shared"
and should also be planned out in a way to be fair.  Got to think about keeping her happy also.  Computers, you can always turn off a switch and step away from for a break...

Just some thoughts I felt I should share with you...

---

First boot off a LiveCD and got to Try...> Go to System > Admin > Disk Utils... Move the first partition back one sector (unit, etc)

Then refer to this thread, section 16,  to install Grub2 on your disk from a LiveCD:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1195275](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1195275)

Let's work on the video first. I believe the nouveau driver is installed by default for the nvidia cards. It is likely that one of the drivers in additional drivers is a driver for the nvidia card. Is that right? My opinion is that if you are having trouble with a device and additional drivers shows another driver for that device, install it from additional drivers. 

Now what other drivers show up? Is one for the wireless device that is crashing? If so, install it. I think you said 3 drivers show up. What is left?

---

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-09
> **Blasphemist said:**
> Let's work on the video first. I believe the nouveau driver is installed by default for the nvidia cards. It is likely that one of the drivers in additional drivers is a driver for the nvidia card. Is that right? My opinion is that if you are having trouble with a device and additional drivers shows another driver for that device, install it from additional drivers. 

[COLOR=Red]Now what other drivers show up?[/COLOR] Is one for the wireless device that is crashing? If so, install it. I think you said 3 drivers show up. What is left?
What drivers?  That was my first thought even before I saw Blassphemist's comment...

I show the video driver should be the 270 series- nvidia-current, nvidia-270... Nouveau's new Nvidia driver might show up as Nvidia Experimental 3D... but not usually on a highend card as this.  Those are drivers that might show up as Additional drivers...  NV, would be Xorg's first pick as an opensource driver, with nouveau and vesa as fallbacks...

---------------------------------
As for the Wireless adapter... Is that card still (used to be) an NDIS wrapper only card with no native Linux support... That didn't really work that well in Windows... with that windows driver? Or has it gotten any better with the newer firmware revisions?  And does it now have it's own Linux Driver?

There is a forum (**[[B]Networking & Wireless**]("http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=336")[/B]) that helps with support for wireless and the NDIS wrapper (If needed)... But as Blasphemous and you said... You suspect that the graphics driver install blew-out or now conflicts with your wireless card...  I don't see where there are any recent reported problems just concerning nviidia and the card in particular.  I'm not doubting you.  I just don't see any reported instance of a graphics driver, breaking this model wireless card.  Maybe it's a new thing that needs to be reported.   Needless, if related, we might be able to help with that-- it's just not my best skill set..

Here- We try to help people with Graphics Issues... So, the most/more important question here, that I don't see you mention at all is-- 

"Does your graphics work?"

You said you picked 3 different graphics drivers, but you didn't mention if any of them worked or how they worked... To me, that seems an important curiousity- especially if there is a suspected conflict somewhere with one of those drivers.  Know what I mean?

Next, if it was something that you installed, that broke it, then could please post the file /var/log/apt/history.log... That may give us an idea what was installed, if there where any errors, conflicts, broken packages, unmet dependencies, held back packages... etc.

Following that-- Here's something to think and to start discussion on about what "might" logically tie these two seemingly separate items together... Nvidia drivers will show up in "Additional Drivers" (aka Jockey).   If his card is now natively supported in Linux (a Linux driver, not a win driver within NDIS) then it would also show in Jockey... If there was a user selection problem or a bug within Jockey...  That's a far reach, but a possibility.

---

Please have a sense of humor about this-  Im a little confused.  Why would you return to something that didn't work (repeatedly)?  Learning and experience is based on hopefully moving forward by not repeating our past mistakes.  Someone once told me that *"Insanity is repeating the same things and expecting different results."*

Of course there's also *"You'll be fine.  I saw this work once in a cartoon"* and *"If it's not broke, maybe you haven't tweaked it enough..."*

But as Blasphemous and you said... You suspect that the graphics driver install blew-out or now conflicts with your wireless card...  I don't see where there are any recent reported problems just concerning nviidia and the card in particular.  I'm not doubting you.  I just don't see any reported instance of a graphics driver, breaking this model wireless card.  Maybe it's a new thing that needs to be reported.   Needless, if related, we might be able to help with that-- it's just not my best skill set..

Here- We try to help people with Graphics Issues... So, the most/more important question here, that I don't see you mention at all is-- 

"Does your graphics work?"

You said you picked 3 different graphics drivers, but you didn't mention if any of them worked or how they worked... To me, that seems an important curiousity- especially if there is a suspected conflict somewhere with one of those drivers.  Know what I mean?

Next, if it was something that you installed, that broke it, then could please post the file /var/log/apt/history.log... That may give us an idea what was installed, if there where any errors, conflicts, broken packages, unmet dependencies, held back packages... etc.

Following that-- Here's something to think and to start discussion on about what "might" logically tie these two seemingly separate items together... Nvidia drivers will show up in "Additional Drivers" (aka Jockey).   If his card is now natively supported in Linux (a Linux driver, not a win driver within NDIS) then it would also show in Jockey... If there was a user selection problem or a bug within Jockey...  That's a far reach, but a possibility.

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-10
> **1timeuse said:**
> these 3 things come up [COLOR=Red]but i tried installing them all[/COLOR] and somehow it just made my wireless not work? dont really know how to explain.. also i cannot update it then send you the file because if i did then i cannot get on the internet and my smart phone crashed with red lights of death so there would be no way to do anything, heres a screen shot of the additional drivers thing and the very last one, the 3d thing is a free license..
Of the 3 drivers I see in your photo, you should use the one that is highlighted (nvidia-current). The first one pictured is the wrong driver and will have you crashed.  The third (Experimental) will work, but it won't get you anything close to that cards capabilities and you'll just be dissatisfied with it.  It does add some things to the lower quality older cards that were made before some of these newer technologies existed.

So which one do you have installed?  If the nividia-173, then there's your problem... If one of the other's then we need to dig deeper.

EDIT- When you said "[COLOR=Red]tried installing them all[/COLOR]' in your post... you meant one at a time, removing the previous before installing the next, right?

And unanswered was if the wireless card was wrapped in the ndiswrapper or did it have a Linux driver for it(?)  I don't see that driver in the the pic you posted from your "Additional Drivers."

I have an idea for that.  I did the same on one of test boxes.  Since it was a test boxes and I get free 10BG hard drives that I would set up with different OS'es or configurations on different drives.  Like you I got tired of continually having to open the case of that box all the time.  It isn't a side opening case. and the way the outside is built, the inside has to slide "out" the back of the outside.  Pain in the....

I went to the computer recyclers and got four mobile drive trays that were the same model.  I installed one of the main trays in a 5 1/4 inch bay and interchange the four cartridges.  This way I can change a drive in a matter of seconds without having to disassemble the case at all.

When you do get back to this...Try adding this kms switch to your boot line
```

acpi_skip_timer_override

```This boot parameter says that:
> 
Recognize and ignore IRQ0/pin2 Interrupt Override.             For broken nForce2 BIOS resulting in XT-PIC timer[FONT=monospace]
I was working on something else and saw that... Thought of you and your hardware and thought it might just help you.
[/FONT]

LOL... Unfortunatley, these graphics challenges are not just going to be with Ubuntu.  The challnges come form changes in Grub2, The kernel and Xorg... So currently, I see these same challenges in other Linux Distro's and Unix... But some Distro's are going off what "IS" proven stable and some what I would say somewhat stable, but new.  

Now that you're testing if other things will run on your's... and knowing your older hardware.  I have some suggestions:

1. Xubuntu or Lubuntu... Still same code as Ubuntu, because they are both Offshoot distro's of Ubuntu, But both have a lot smaller footprint hardware and resource wise.  I think Kubuntu might not be a good choice until we figure out what is going on with your install... It comes form Ubuntu, but has a bigger footprint than Ubuntu and is a bit demanding on graphics.

2. OpenIndiana... Very stable code and codebase.  Not Linux.  Unix.

3. Liquid Lemur.  Linux.  Very good scripting on that distro's install scripts... <--Some of the very best I've seen!  From my testing , still in beta, but very stable.

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-19
> **cheffe77 said:**
> Hi,

I have recently upgrade to 11.04 from 10.10. Since that time my laptop starts with a black screen (only grub is displayed). Starting in rescue mode the system hangs at [0.088004]. 
Unfortunately, it is not possible to read what the actual problem is as the error messages pass so quickly and the break key is not working. There is no way to switch to console using <ctrl> <alt> <f1>. 

What I tried so far:

1. set grub to: vga=771
2. set radeonmode=1

But it did not change anything. 

Try instead to delete "quiet splash" and replace with " --verbose text " which should let it boot the kernel with verbose messaging.. into a text console mode.  That will verfify that the kernel is booting and let you review and errors, with <Shift><PgUp> and <Shift><PgDwn>...

After you get to a command line, look at and post your /var/log/xorg.1.log... which should be one log back from the present (0).

Post the results from 
```

sudo lspci -v | grep VGA

```

---

### Post by cheffe77 on 2011-07-21
continued from #495

Hi,
thanks, I appreciate your help very much. I tried --verbose text, but the system does not start. It hangs at [0.088004]. I also reinstalled the kernel, but no change.


sudo lspci -v | grep VGA returned:
01:05.0 VGA compatible controller: ATI Technologies Inc Radeon XPRESS 200M 5955 (PCIE) (prog-if 00 [VGA controller])

---

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-21
> **cheffe77 said:**
> continued from #495

Hi,
thanks, I appreciate your help very much. I tried --verbose text, but the system does not start. [COLOR=Red]It hangs at [0.088004][/COLOR]. I also reinstalled the kernel, but no change.


sudo lspci -v | grep VGA returned:
01:05.0 VGA compatible controller: ATI Technologies Inc Radeon XPRESS 200M 5955 (PCIE) (prog-if 00 [VGA controller])


cheers
The card is supported and a startup option ("IF" the kernel would boot) would be " radeon.mode=1 "... But see below...

Unfortunately 
> 
I tried --verbose text, but the system does not start.
 ...it telling you that the kernel is not booting into a text console mode, which is as basic as it gets- besides a rescue mode which would be via a " rescue " boot option.

> [COLOR=Red]It hangs at [0.088004][/COLOR].The number you quoted is a time stamp... should have had some text after or before this to say what step its trying to do.  Without seeing that text, I'd have reinstalled the kernel, but I see you did try that already (good catch on that).  

No sense in going on trying to get graphics going while the kernel is not booting yet.  Are you sure your were completely mounted to you installed system and change rooted to it --> when you tried to reinstall the kernel?  No offense intended / just have to ask to rule that out.

What does it say in your dmsg log?  (found in /var/log/) You'd think that something that drastic should show up there and be easier to find than going through the syslog...  Then /var/log/dpkg.log should have info on the kernel reinstall, to check if there was any errors during that...

dpkg.log:

2011-07-23 13:25:04 startup archives unpack

### Post by cheffe77 on 2011-07-23
Alrigth, the problem is solved. I installed kernel 2.6.38-10-generic incl headers. Now everything works perfectely.

Thanks for the help.

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-23
> **nobackstab1 said:**
> Dear All, 
 I have read a lot of reviews but could not fix the problem I am having. I am using a HP Pavillion dv6000 with partitions dual boot: vista and Ubuntu 11.4. I have upgraded my Ubuntu 11.4 to Gnome 3 and after rebooting I am getting a blank screen with mountall: Plymouth command failed next line mountall : Disconnected from Plymouth. 

* Starting anac(h)ronistic crom                      [ok]
* Stopping anac(h)ronistic crom                      [ok]
* Checking battery state...                          [ok]
* Stopping System V runlevel compatibility           [ok]
mounthall: Plymouth command failed 
              mounthall: Disconnected from Plymouth
                                                   -
And a blank screen

My graphic is Nvidia card.. I tried uninstalling but that did not help..

Any advice would be appriciated..

Many thanks
Prashant
This is a fix from launchpad.  It will force the graphic drivers to hit before mountall package.  This is only to get plymouth to start and nothing else.  If it isn't that particular probleml and you get a splash, this fix will not cause any problems leabing it in.
```
sudo -i
``````
echo FRAMEBUFFER=y > /etc/initramfs-tools/conf.d/splash
update-initramfs -u
```
This fix is in this thread and was used successfully for this problem.

---

### Post by arnm7890 on 2011-07-23
Hi there,

This is my first installation of Ubuntu, so I am a complete noob.
I installed natty using a LiveCD, which worked fine. However, when I tried to boot Ubuntu via GRUB, i got the black screen and nothing else.
I followed the instructions on this sticky, ran the text based console, which all worked fine. However, I arrived at a problem when I tried to run failsafe mode. I received an error message saying "Problem with configuration server - gconf sanity check 2 exited", and I was not able to login even through failsafe. I searched online for a long time for a solution to this, many websites and forums suggested it was a permissions problem, however, changing the permissions as outlined (e.g. chmod 1777 /tmp, chmod 775 /etc/gconf/gconf.xml.system, etc) did not work for me.
I am really at a loss now as to what I should do. I need Linux for University (I am a second year Software Engineering student) and I dont know how to proceed.
Any help will be greatly appreciated.

Many thanks.

EDIT> Solved. Did a fresh install of Ubuntu with more space on the partition

---

### Post by 007casper on 2011-07-25
your nvidia thread was super.  I cant thank you enough!!! Cheers!

I tried all variables one at a time.
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash nomodeset acpi_osi=\"Linux\"
and it didnt work.

but for me vga=0x101 worked.  It posts a funky message during boot.  I got to find out what it says during boot.  It also boots slower than before.  However, it works.  Thats what matters.

GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash vga=0x101"
```

GRUB> echo $linux_gfx_mode
echo $gfxmode
 set gfxmode=2560x1600
load_video (this line didnt work for me)
insmod gfxterm
 GRUB> vbeinfo

```
append/adding a vga=xxx, where xxx is the mode. 

thank you.

---

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-26
> **007casper said:**
> your nvidia thread was super.  I cant thank you enough!!! Cheers!

I tried all variables one at a time.
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash nomodeset [COLOR=Red]acpi_osi=\"Linux\"[/COLOR]
and it didnt work.

but for me vga=0x101 worked.  It posts a funky message during boot.  I got to find out what it says during boot.  It also boots slower than before.  However, it works.  Thats what matters.

GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash vga=0x101"
```

GRUB> echo $linux_gfx_mode
echo $gfxmode
 set gfxmode=2560x1600
load_video (this line didnt work for me)
insmod gfxterm
 GRUB> vbeinfo

```append/adding a vga=xxx, where xxx is the mode. 

thank you.
You're welcome... Note on the [COLOR=Red]acpi_osi=[/COLOR] switch... Looks like you found that in one of the later posts after the 3 post...  There not a problem with that switch, but rather with a bug in grub2 and how it applies quotation marks.  

How it appears and how it is supposed to be to the kernel is: acpi="Operating System To Mimic Name".  Grub2 strips out the question marks and the kernel would then see of that parameter as param=acpi_osi with a value=Operating, param=System with value=to (errors), param=Mimic with value-Name (errors)..l 

Somewhere's after that post, Jim and I played with this switch and tried to use escape characters, whch Grub2 didn't recognize either.  I finally compiles a kernel with all the debugging symbols in it to see what was going on and well...

Since "Linux" in one singular word (no spaces), it didn't matter if the quotation mark were missing if you remove them in that switch... So the correct is:
```

acpi_osi=Linux

```I had updated the first post (page 1), but didn't go through the later posts to correct them.  I'm sorry possible confusion on that...

---

### Post by Blasphemist on 2011-07-27
Thanks for furthering our work on that dang parameter Mike. Where did you end up finding this? I've been looking in depth into multi-boot for grub2 so I've looked at lots of documentation and haven't seen this. 

I've been using the back slash escape character to get "Linux" into the grub config file on my own box. Does that get interpreted the same as without quotes?

---

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-27
> **Blasphemist said:**
> Thanks for furthering our work on that dang parameter Mike. Where did you end up finding this? I've been looking in depth into multi-boot for grub2 so I've looked at lots of documentation and haven't seen this. 

I've been using the back slash escape character to get "Linux" into the grub config file on my own box. Does that get interpreted the same as without quotes?
[COLOR=Teal]acpi_osi=Linux[/COLOR]  or [COLOR=Teal]acpi_osi="Linux"[/COLOR] will both end up as [COLOR=Teal]param=acpi-_osi[/COLOR] with [COLOR=Teal]value=Linux[/COLOR]...

[COLOR=Green]acpi_osi=\"Linux\"[/COLOR] ended up as a [COLOR=Green]value=\Linux\[/COLOR] and [COLOR=Green]acpi_osi=/"Linux/" [/COLOR]as [COLOR=Teal]value=/Linux/[/COLOR]... You can see that adding an escape character didn't work.  You can verify this by looking at your xorg.x.log, which records the kernel boot line- and it will show what was used, without having to use a special debugging kernel to trace it.

You were the one that got me going on finding this out, Jim.  With debugging symbols compiled in, I set breaks and traced/watched the parameters and values it was passing (tedious), were I could see what was going on...  Once I found that out, it showed it wasn't just the os_prober, like we originally thought, but Grub2 itself.

I went upstream to GNU Grub's Bug Tracker (Savannah), thinking we were on to something "new"... Seemed to be a known problem that is already reported up there. (LOL) I really never realized that it wasn't working (as it should have) until you pointed it out to me.

For "us" it's a non-problem, because if we use "Linux" or Linux, that one word parameter gets passed the same.  For users that need to pass specific  Windows versions, something like "!Windows 2006", Grub2's stripping the quotation marks out and having that space in there-> is going to cause an error, because of the two words and no way to encapsulate/pass a multiple word string as a value.

[COLOR=Green]acpi_osi=\'Linux\"[/COLOR] ended up as a [COLOR=Green]value=\Linux\[/COLOR] and [COLOR=Green]acpi_osi=/"Linux/" [/COLOR]as [COLOR=Teal]value=/Linux/[/COLOR]... With debugging symbols compiled in, I set breaks and traced/watched the parameters and values it was passing (tedious), were I could see what was going on...  Once I found that out, it showed it wasn't just the os_prober, like we originally thought, but Grub2 itself.

I went upstream to GNU Grub's Bug Tracker (Savannah), thinking we were on to something "new"... Seemed to be a known problem that is already reported up there. (LOL) I really never realized that it wasn't working (as it should have) until you pointed it out to me.

For "us" it's a non-problem, because if we use "Linux" or Linux, that one word parameter gets passed the same.  For users that need to pass specific  Windows versions, something like "!Windows 2006", Grub2's stripping the quotation marks out and having that space in there-> is going to cause an error, because of the two words and no way to encapsulate/pass a multiple word string as a value.

Mike- Sure does seem hard to get to the bottom of this for such a small thing. I do however have different results than you're reporting, I think. 

In my etc/default/grub file the line is this:
> GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash acpi_osi=\"Linux\""
This of course gets used by grub-mkconfig that is run by update-grub (along with the scripts in /etc/grub.d) to create the /boot/grub.cfg setting. Here is the result from my grub.cfg file.
> linux	/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-10-generic root=UUID=674654cd-01b0-4dc2-9f8e-f80278b5005d ro   quiet splash acpi_osi="Linux" vt.handoff=7
As you can see, it looks to me like the \ escape characters properly created the desired parenthesis bracketed parameter. Now whether or not this is properly passed on from here, I don't know. This is the result in my Xorg.0.log.
> Kernel command line: BOOT_IMAGE=/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-10-generic root=UUID=674654cd-01b0-4dc2-9f8e-f80278b5005d ro quiet splash acpi_osi=Linux vt.handoff=7 

It seems to me this is processed as needed and as would be required for multi word parameters by grub at least as far as the boot command line. I can't speak to the use of this by X but I'm wondering if there is a problem handling this that it may be in X. What do you think?

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-07-29
> **Blasphemist said:**
> (edited)
This of course gets used by [COLOR=Green]grub-mkconfig[/COLOR] that is run by update-grub (along with the scripts in /etc/grub.d) to create the /boot/grub.cfg setting. Here is the result from my grub.cfg file.

As you can see, it looks to me like the \ escape characters properly created the desired parenthesis bracketed parameter. 

On the parsor of grub-mkconfig- yes.  It's taking the escape  character... Like you and I had found before... But Grub2 itself  doesn't.  Look at the kernel boot line of yours below... it stripped the quotation marks out.  The problem I was getting slammed by users by > If they tried to add the escape characters via a grub menu edit > Then grub was passing a partial escape character and failing (grub-mkconfig recognises tham <> grub2 doesn't.)
> **Blasphemist said:**
> Now whether or not this is properly passed on from here, I don't know. This is the result in my Xorg.0.log.
```
Kernel command line: BOOT_IMAGE=/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-10-generic  root=UUID=674654cd-01b0-4dc2-9f8e-f80278b5005d ro quiet splash  [COLOR=Red]acpi_osi=Linux [/COLOR]vt.handoff=7                      
```
So "We" (you & I) know what is going on with it <> confusing to users we have to pass info on to about it.  If we just say "forget the quotation mark in that switch"  for using apci_osi=Linux... It ends up the same to the kernel...  Because Grub2 is going to strip out those quotation marks anyways.
> **Blasphemist said:**
> It seems to me this is processed as needed and as would be required for multi word parameters by grub at least as far as the boot command line. I can't speak to the use of this by X but I'm wondering if there is a problem handling this that it may be in X. What do you think?If X needs that switch, as far as I can tell from the param= and value= that the kernel takes and passes to X... a single word still comes out correct.

Like I said, for just the single word Linux, we can do it as we want (Linux or "LInux") and it will end up the same.... We could do overkill of \"Linux]" if only in the /etc/default/grub, becuase it is processed by grub-mkconfig, which after all is said and done, is going to end up as the previous 2 methods...

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-08-07
Not really a graphics issue, but can be eaily mistaken as one (because they go to blackscreens):

Re: Ubuntu Server Edition Install disk, Freezes and Blackscreen Issues.

For some reason, if the hardware (especially floppy drive) fails and the BIOS settings are set to something that the disk sees diffrent... there is blackscreen and system lockup issues at different parts of the install.

On the floppy issue- The disk will lockup at a blackscreen on initial boot of the Install disk.  

On other drives, if there is a problem with a drive  that is in the primary BIOS or in a Hardware RAID BIOS, when you select install from the main menu, it will then lockup at the next screen with a blackscreen.

It installing with RAID and there is a problem with the RAID definition, it will lock up at the partitioning application init...

---

### Post by Lord C on 2011-08-20
I seem to be having the same problem as many... I've just fresh installed Ubuntu 11.04, the LiveCD runs fine yet, but after install my monitor displays a black screen stating "Cannot display this video mode". I've tried CTRL + ALT + F1 - 6 but nothing happens.

It's not booting Grub for some reason. 

Following this thread, I've tried commenting out GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT=0 and changing GRUG_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT to = "quiet splash vga=775"

Tried GRUB_GFXPAYLOAD_LINUX=text

Tried installing the whole nvidia-current and running nvidia-xconfig from [page 1]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10740097&postcount=3").

I've also tried booting from the LiveCD and selecting nomodeset etc. to no effect.

There's a lot of information in this thread, and I'm trying to take it all in and figure out what's useful in my case. Just going through a lot of trial and error, but I've been at it all day now and would really appreciate if anyone could point me in the right direction :)

My monitor's optimal resolution is 1280x1024 and I have an old nvidia graphics card (7600GT).

[**Update**]
I missed a key piece of information among all the text:

After making changed to /etc/default/grub, you need to run
sudo update-grub

I did this after mounting the hard drive (as per nvidia tutorial on page one)

Grub now boots into text more. \o/

### Post by Wraiths82 on 2011-08-23

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-08-24
> **Wraiths82 said:**
> Hello,
 
I am having the same blinking cursor problem but none of the short cut keys work for me including shift.  I'm pretty new to this so could you please explain how I edit the grub when I can't get to anything?
 
Thanks in advance
 
James
Here's some instructions I wrote for someone else to tweak Grub2 when it wants to "hide."  They will work for you also. You may have to boot from a LiveCD and go to a terminal to edit the files to do this...
> **MAFoElffen said:**
> Next, I have users that go to my graphics sticky and said they couldn't  get to a grub menu via holding down a shift key during boot.  I didn't  know if to believe them at first, but  was wrong... Well, this test box  of mine is one of those in that respect, so was a good test case for trying to  get this working... 

Here's what my tests with that come up-- with values of the file /etc/default, lines --> GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT_QUIET=false:
 # --and line--
GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT="x"[FONT=monospace]
[/FONT]
Where the values of x are:
value = 0 --> skips over the menu / doesn't display on this box- even while holding down <Shift> Very frustrating...

value = any positive number, such as 10 --> Timer displays and counts  down for the value set.  Screen is black with the timer displaying in  the upper left of screen.  Pressing any key while the timer is present  brings up the menu.  This works, but still...

value = "" (meaning blank after the equals sign) > Displays the grub  menu everytime without any user interaction.  Wahoo!  Success. (Written  as [FONT=monospace]"[/FONT]GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT=   ")

It seems to work great... 
Hope this gets you further along...

### Post by g12345567 on 2011-08-30
I also have the same bootup problem when installing Ubuntu 11.04 AMD 64 desktop. The same think happened when I tried to install XBMC live which runs Ubuntu 10.1.

It just boots up up with a blank screen after a sucessfull installation. And the only option available is to press Ctrl+Alt+Del and reboot the pc.

My PC:

HP microserver 
Processor: AMD athlon II - Neo N36L 1.3Ghz (Dual core 64bit)
Hard drive: HP 250gb (Seagate 7200.12 with HP firmware)

Turns out that the boot loader was being installed to the 2nd hdd I had in my machine, which was formatted as NTFS. Ended up disconnecting the ntfs hdd and reinstalling ubuntu 11.04. After that, everything booted up fine. No more hanging cursor o startup

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-09-15
> **miroi said:**
> Dear all,

On my AMD64,NVidiaGF9200 I upgraded Ubuntu to 11.04. Got the same problem - violet blank screen during boot.


I played intensively with /etc/default/grub file, neither parameter helped - still got either blank screen, or blicking cursor...I am giving up this way.


Would you please tell how to downgrade grub easily (as text command) to prevent this boot failure ?

Thanks, Miro
Did you get through the Trouble Shooting Flow Chart to confirm that the kernel can boot to a tty text console screen... to confirm that is a problem with X?  If so then when you go to the next step (in your case with nvidia) to add "nomodeset" temporarily as a kernel boot option, does it work?  (These steps are all covered in post 1 of this thread)

If this still doesn't get you anywhere, then we need to look at your /var/log/xorg.0.log (please post) to see that (in your case) your nvidiia video driver is loading correctly.

It helps to ask any kind of questions about things to get help... Answers will be helpful to you.  I am not psychic.  But this thread is intended to answer peoples questions, to help them to solve their graphics issues.

---

### Post by miroi on 2011-09-17
Dear [MAFoElffen]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=1044547"),

> **MAFoElffen said:**
> Did you get through the Trouble Shooting Flow Chart to confirm that the kernel can boot to a tty text console screen... to confirm that is a problem with X?  If so then when you go to the next step (in your case with nvidia) to add "nomodeset" temporarily as a kernel boot option, does it work?  (These steps are all covered in post 1 of this thread).

finally, I made it: based on "sudo hwinfo --frame" output I set GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="vga=0x037d" and no blank screen at boot ! Perfeckt ! I returned to the nVidia proprietary driver 280.13 without further problems (before I had "nouveau" open driver).

Well, to conclude, this ill-behaviour is caused by the fresh grub 1.99~rc1-13ubuntu3. 

Anyhow, thank for all your work you made on this topic. Best,

Miro

---

finally, I made it: based on "sudo hwinfo --frame" output I set GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="vga=0x037d" and no blank screen at boot ! Perfeckt ! I returned to the nVidia proprietary driver 280.13 without further problems (before I had "nouveau" open driver).

Well, to conclude, this ill-behaviour is caused by the fresh grub 1.99~rc1-13ubuntu3. 

Anyhow, thank for all your work you made on this topic. Best,

Miro
Good to hear the info helped...

It's a combination of the changes made to grub, the kernel and xorg to implement KMS technologies... Translation?  They are trying to get graphics technologies in the Linux OS to catch up to the capabilities of todays GPU's... There's a few growing pains in those adjustments for that.

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-09-22
> **TweFoju said:**
> Hi

this is not a resolution post, and sorry if i am asking this on the wrong thread
but i just want to ask

i will have a laptop with GTX 560m GPU soon, and i have been reading quite a few people say that we should get the driver from the sudo terminal instead from the Nvidia website

why is that?

because isn't it better just to download from Nvidia since it's certified and it supports linux
so why would we want not to download it from Nvidia?

Depends who you ask... and that changes also.  I feel it's a personal decision and preference.

Easiest is to install the NVidia driver packages from Ubuntu for use with Ubuntu. More work is to install the binary drivers from NVidia.

The Ubuntu drivers are derived from the NVidia Binary files, but change a little and evolve from there as related to Ubuntu itself.  There is a bit of a deley from there, meaning there is a guy at Conical who gets the binary on it's release date, packages it in xswat... the drivers there are bleeding-edge new are those who use them have to expect that there may be problems...

Once they get tested a few times they get moved to the edgers ppa... Still expect a few problems, but less.  Then they get move to the stable repos.  Some problems get through.

The NVidia Binaries from NVidia sometimes have no problems at release with Ubuntu.  Those that are, end up in their bug system and are prioritized to their own priorities.  The advantages is that you can tweak thngs a little finer (IMHO) with the binaries and NVidia's tools.  I can tell you that one of the bugs with the newest of the binaries at this date is that Unity does not come up...  They recognize that there are issues.

I've ran for years with both with little problems on various hardware combo's and versions.  I assist people who have problems with either or both.  The advantage here, is that you have a choice to use either and decide what is best for you.

A Head's up on the near future- Xorg-Xserver 1.11 is out for testing... and has issues.  It's not in Ubuntu stable yet... and that IS for reason.  I can tell you that as it relates to NVidia, that the Ubuntu packaged drivers work, but the proprietary binaries from NVidia do not.  We'll see what changes they make to try to fix this.

---

### Post by lucasfigueiredo on 2011-09-30
hi, I've been looking at different threads for days and have gotten more confused and overwhelmed with the variety of bugs than I was able to do anything.
I have the same problems as a lot of people, but two special conditions that may be why I can't solve it.
I've recently used wubi.exe to install Natty over my Win7 so I wouldn't have to mess with formatting and partitions once again (lazy). That's one of my special conditions, I'm not sure as to what extend that makes me different than others.
The other thing is I'm on a Vaio Z, and I have 2 video cards, an Intel chipset and a Nvidia GT330M (not really sure of the model).
On Windows I select which I'm going to use via a physical switch on my laptop. In ubuntu it doesn't even light up, so I'm assuming it's using the Nvidia one, I'll explain why in a second.
When I first loaded I had Unity, and I tried using CCSM to get the cube even before updating to the proprietary drivers. that caused a bad crash and all I could do was uninstall everything through tty1 and use gnome classic.
I then installed the drivers Janitor told me to, and upon opening nvidia-settings I was "invited" to run nvidia-xconfig.
I think I'm running Nvidia because Janitor told me to download the drivers. But when nvidia-xconfig rewrites my xorg.conf, and I restart gdm, I get a blank screen. I then have to either reload xorg.conf.failsafe or remove xorg.conf and restart X.
That gets me back to gnome classic.

And that's where I am right now, not really knowing which card I'm using, and not being able to configure my drivers - thus being stuck with gnome classic.

TL;DR: I have a Vaio Z with 2 graph cards, I don't know how to switch between them and I can't get my nvidia driver to work.


Hope I was clear enough, please ask me about any more info on this problem.
Thanks a lot

Some parts will display, but when I open some things, my browser for instance, I just get a white window with just the unity top menu above.

Boot into safe graphics to post the results of these:
```

lspci -v | grep VGA

```Will tell us which NVidia Card you have...

If you can post your /var/log/Xorg.1.log

This was tossed as a fault of either the GPU driver or of compiz... On some, rolling back the video driver back to something older solved it.  On others, reconfiguring compiz solved it.  After getting the above results, we'll look further into what's happenign on your box.

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-09-30
Still waiting for feedback... Assuming you have at least a GeoForce 6 series Nvidia card.  Anything lower would not run unity.

What video driver are your using?  Look in Additional Drivers...

If you are using nvidia-current, try removing it (using the Additional drivers app), then select and install the nvidia-173 driver in it's place.

Reboot and check it out.

### Post by lucasfigueiredo on 2011-10-01
> **TweFoju said:**
> has anyone actually succeeded in using bumblebee?

as in like, you can switch between Intel and nVidia

because i know how to switch the graphics using a command + bumblebee

but there is no way to tell if it switched

I've read somewhere you have to look at the time left while on battery, it's supposed to change drastically.

I've been able "switch" between them using acpi call or something like that, but nothing really changes.

What does modprobe do?
I've done it to nvidia-current and it "worked" but the only thing that changed was that after "turning it off" using acpi call modprobe wont work anymore until the next boot.

---

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-10-01
> **lucasfigueiredo said:**
> can OP please help? I posted earlier today, but since then I've narrowed my problem down to the hybrid graphics my laptop has.
I've searched and found something called ironhide (former bumblebee) and have failed to configure it.
Ideally I'd like to be able to choose between my cards and use Unity with at least the nVidia one.
I'd be okay with being able to enable the nVidia once and for all though...

Does anyone have any experience with hybrid graphics??
Very little... and we coverd some of it in earlier posts.  (@Jim- remember phil?) 

Some of these have have a BIOS Option to be able toselect which card.  Others that do not have that BIOS Optin, but sometimes can use a switcheroo option in the acpi of the kernel...

If you can post the results of:
```
[FONT=monospace]
[/FONT]grep -i switcheroo /boot/config-2.6.*

```That will tells us if that support is available to you.

Ironhide. previously Bumblebee... my understanding is that "it" is a script that calls through to acpi to the switcheroo functions in the kernel.  You can call these function manually.  If they work manually for you, then I can figure out how to add custom menu items to automate it for you.
> **lucasfigueiredo said:**
> I've read somewhere you have to look at  the time left while on battery, it's supposed to change drastically.

I've been able "switch" between them using acpi call or something like that, but nothing really changes.

What does modprobe do?
I've done it to nvidia-current and it "worked" but the only thing that  changed was that after "turning it off" using acpi call modprobe wont  work anymore until the next boot.
Yes... That's the whole theory and selling point of hybrid graphics on laptops.  That while in a normal mode, where highend graphics are "not needed", that you can save battery.  In normal supported driver mode, it detects the graphics and autmatedly switches between the two cards on demand. (saving power when it can) Without a supported driver, If you leave it on Highend graphics, it's going to have more power consumption.

modprobe forces a load on a module to the system base files.  After that forced load... yes, next boot.

### Post by lucasfigueiredo on 2011-10-01
> **MAFoElffen said:**
> Very little... and we coverd some of it in earlier posts.  (@Jim- remember phil?) 

Some of these have have a BIOS Option to be able toselect which card.  Others that do not have that BIOS Optin, but sometimes can use a switcheroo option in the acpi of the kernel...

If you can post the results of:
```
[FONT=monospace]
[/FONT]grep -i switcheroo /boot/config-2.6.*

```That will tells us if that support is available to you.

Ironhide. previously Bumblebee... my understanding is that "it" is a script that calls through to acpi to the switcheroo functions in the kernel.  You can call these function manually.  If they work manually for you, then I can figure out how to add custom menu items to automate it for you.

Yes... That's the whole theory and selling point of hybrid graphics on laptops.  That while in a normal mode, where highend graphics are "not needed", that you can save battery.  In normal supported driver mode, it detects the graphics and autmatedly switches between the two cards on demand. (saving power when it can) Without a supported driver, If you leave it on Highend graphics, it's going to have more power consumption.

modprobe forces a load on a module to the system base files.  After that forced load... yes, next boot.

```
grep -i switcheroo /boot/config-2.6*
/boot/config-2.6.38-11-generic:CONFIG_VGA_SWITCHEROO=y
/boot/config-2.6.38-8-generic:CONFIG_VGA_SWITCHEROO=y
```

seems like I have it..
but does that mean my nvidia card is working? the drivers don't seem to be working, maybe that's where I need help.

I think I'm running Unity on my crappy on-board card, but compiz --replace crashes everything (freezes and when I ctrl^C my windows are borderless and all screwed up).

If I can get the effects to work (or even some of them so I can show my gf how cool linux can be for girls lol) on the low-end card, that's fine.
I think the only time I'll need the good card is for HDMI support...

That may be where your problem lies... Suggested (I think it's more a requirement!) on uninstalling and purging the previous nVidia drivers before you install a new one (same thing for ATI Radeon drivers).

I wrote up some instructions for that and installing nVidia's binary drivers back in post  			#[**280**]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10909540&postcount=280") (<-- Follow link).

***Before you do that, do this:
```

lspci -nn | grep VGA
[code]
Which is going to have ouput similar to this:
[code]
mafoelffen@maf-ubuntu-01:~$ sudo lspci -nn | grep VGA
03:00.0 VGA compatible controller [0300]: nVidia Corporation G92 [GeForce 9800 GTX] [10de:[COLOR=Red]0612[/COLOR]] (rev a2)
```
See the [COLOR=Red]highlighted hex[/COLOR] number above?  Copy it down (adding "0x" in front of it) and go to nVidia to this link-- [Here]("http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86/270.41.19/README/supportedchips.html")

Use that hex ID number to find your card in that list, it will tell you what series driver you should use for your card...

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-10-02
> **TweFoju said:**
> i will have a laptop with GTX 560m GPU soon, and i have been reading quite a few people say that we should get the driver from the sudo terminal instead from the Nvidia website
Sorry-- It was 3 in the morning here when I answered your last post and I thought I was answering "lucasfigueiredo"... After seeing "your" name on that this morning and this:
> also, i think right now my nVidia is active using BumblebeeSo both of you have similar switched graphics and are running bumblebee(?) The instructions I gave you is still valid and I need that info from both of you..

Long Range plan for you both?  Seems bumblebee had said that they supported both Ubuntu's nvidia-current and the binary drivers from nVidia... But in the evolution of Bumblebee to Ironhide say they now only recommend and support with nVidia's binary drivers.

So the recommended choice would be to install the binary nVidia drivers from nVidia. (Instructions in Post #[**280**]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10909540&postcount=280") of this thread)... I guess I should research more on the "Ironhide" installation and troubleshooting (Jim also) as this is what it seems is going to be the future migration for you two and the direction you 2 need to go.  

Unfortunately, even though they share files and modules, Ironhide says you should uninstall bumblebee before installing Ironhide...   Which is a good thing..

I'm researching this to come up with a solution for you two and to use as a guide for others.  

_Sidenote_
Yesterday, Jim and I bounced ideas off each other and talked about this.  We where thinking about creating our own "PY/GTK+ GUI based Application" to do this, with the logic of how we would do it, the features and abilities it would have- wrapped in a GUI kind of presentation, with a "name" that was somehow related and descriptive of the application. We looked into Ironhide and Bumblebee and what they were "already" doing. I looked through it's code and that's what and basically that is how they are already doing things (same ideas). If we did put something together, it would turn out to be an alternative, doing the same thing, with the same features(?)  :Thinking Out Loud: (Not closing the door on this yet... maybe)

First, thanks to all who have posted and discussed on this thread so far. Special thanks to MAFoElffen. Your initial posts #1 & #2, as well as your [[COLOR="Red"]post #280[/COLOR]]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10909540&postcount=280") are most informative.

Hardware: Asus P4P800 motherboard, P4-3.0Mhz, 2.0 Gb RAM, primary IDE hdd 20.4Gb as /, a secondary SATA hdd 2.0Tb (split into /home (500Gb) & /var 1.5Tb) and an Nvidia GeForce 6200 video with DVI-out, VGA-out and SVIDEO-out.

Software: A fresh install of Ubuntu-server 11.04 with OpenSSH, and Mythbuntu (front end, back end & additional roles)

Monitor: An old Princeton EO500 (for setup purposes only.)

I have gone to Nvidia's website and downloaded and installed the latest driver (280.13.) per the instructions of post #280. I now have a functional GUI. However, the driver is not "seeing" the Princeton monitor. Nor is the driver "activated" per Jockey. However Nvidia-settings indicates that the driver is present. (Just a little concerned (scared, actually) to try activating the driver, but I might have too.)

```
~$ sudo hwinfo --monitor
[sudo] password for user: 
> hal.1: read hal dataprocess 2526: arguments to dbus_move_error() were incorrect, assertion "(dest) == NULL || !dbus_error_is_set ((dest))" failed in file dbus-errors.c line 280.
This is normally a bug in some application using the D-Bus library.
libhal.c 3483 : Error unsubscribing to signals, error=The name org.freedesktop.Hal was not provided by any .service files
28: None 00.0: 10000 Monitor                                    
  [Created at fb.71]
  Unique ID: rdCR.EY_qmtb9YY0
  Hardware Class: monitor
  Model: "Generic Monitor"
  Vendor: "Generic"
  Device: "Monitor"
  Resolution: 1024x768@76Hz
  Driver Info #0:
    Max. Resolution: 1024x768
    Vert. Sync Range: 50-90 Hz
    Hor. Sync Range: 31-61 kHz
  Config Status: cfg=new, avail=yes, need=no, active=unknown
```

Grated this says "Resolution: 1024x768@76Hz" but, the monitor is running a 640x480.

```
sudo lshw -c Display
  *-display               
       description: VGA compatible controller
       product: NV44A [GeForce 6200]
       vendor: nVidia Corporation
       physical id: 0
       bus info: pci@0000:01:00.0
       version: a1
       width: 32 bits
       clock: 66MHz
       capabilities: pm agp agp-3.0 vga_controller bus_master cap_list rom
       configuration: driver=nvidia latency=248 maxlatency=1 mingnt=5

```

```
cat /etc/default/grub
# If you change this file, run 'update-grub' afterwards to update
# /boot/grub/grub.cfg.
# For full documentation of the options in this file, see:
#   info -f grub -n 'Simple configuration'

GRUB_DEFAULT=0
#GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT=0
GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT_QUIET=true
GRUB_TIMEOUT=8
GRUB_DISTRIBUTOR=`lsb_release -i -s 2> /dev/null || echo Debian`
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash acpi_osi=\"Linux\" vga=792"
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=""
#GRUB_GFXPAYLOAD=text

# Uncomment to enable BadRAM filtering, modify to suit your needs
# This works with Linux (no patch required) and with any kernel that obtains
# the memory map information from GRUB (GNU Mach, kernel of FreeBSD ...)
#GRUB_BADRAM="0x01234567,0xfefefefe,0x89abcdef,0xefefefef"

# Uncomment to disable graphical terminal (grub-pc only)
#GRUB_TERMINAL=console

# The resolution used on graphical terminal
# note that you can use only modes which your graphic card supports via VBE
# you can see them in real GRUB with the command `vbeinfo'
GRUB_GFXMODE=1024x768x24

# Uncomment if you don't want GRUB to pass "root=UUID=xxx" parameter to Linux
#GRUB_DISABLE_LINUX_UUID=true

# Uncomment to disable generation of recovery mode menu entries
#GRUB_DISABLE_RECOVERY="true"

# Uncomment to get a beep at grub start
#GRUB_INIT_TUNE="480 440 1"
```

So, I am not sure how to proceed with solving my resolution issue. This box is intended to become a Mythbuntu box in my living room to test and experiment with. The end result will be this card connected via S-Video cable to our television. I have connected it (temporarily) to the television and the resolution is sable (doesn't roll) but, slightly fuzzy and unreadable. Mostly (I think) due to the size of the television (32") and the incorrect resolution. (It may have been running at 1024x768 on the TV but< I could not read the fonts to be certain.) So, I have moved back to my Princeton (antique) monitor in an effort to resolve this resolution issue.

### Post by lucasfigueiredo on 2011-10-02
> **MAFoElffen said:**
> Sorry-- It was 3 in the morning here when I answered your last post and I thought I was answering "lucasfigueiredo"... After seeing "your" name on that this morning and this:
So both of you have similar switched graphics and are running bumblebee(?) The instructions I gave you is still valid and I need that info from both of you..

Long Range plan for you both?  Seems bumblebee had said that they supported both Ubuntu's nvidia-current and the binary drivers from nVidia... But in the evolution of Bumblebee to Ironhide say they now only recommend and support with nVidia's binary drivers.

So the recommended choice would be to install the binary nVidia drivers from nVidia. (Instructions in Post #[**280**]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10909540&postcount=280") of this thread)... I guess I should research more on the "Ironhide" installation and troubleshooting (Jim also) as this is what it seems is going to be the future migration for you two and the direction you 2 need to go.  

Unfortunately, even though they share files and modules, Ironhide says you should uninstall bumblebee before installing Ironhide...   Which is a good thing..

I'm researching this to come up with a solution for you two and to use as a guide for others.  

_Sidenote_
Yesterday, Jim and I bounced ideas off each other and talked about this.  We where thinking about creating our own "PY/GTK+ GUI based Application" to do this, with the logic of how we would do it, the features and abilities it would have- wrapped in a GUI kind of presentation, with a "name" that was somehow related and descriptive of the application. We looked into Ironhide and Bumblebee and what they were "already" doing. I looked through it's code and that's what and basically that is how they are already doing things (same ideas). If we did put something together, it would turn out to be an alternative, doing the same thing, with the same features(?)  :Thinking Out Loud: (Not closing the door on this yet... maybe)

:oops:

so...
I have installed ironhide (w/o configuring) but I don't know how to switch between cards or even how to tell which one I'm using now.
My Xorg.conf is pretty much empty (I think I've deleted it even) because when I try to add "Driver " lines, my X doesn't start.
below is my Xorg.0.log for what it's worth.

I've been doing some reading and I've found a BIOS hack that will allow me to switch between cards via hardware. I'm not really comfortable doing that, I wish someone who has successfully used ironhide on a Vaio Z would share their experience. Does anyone know how to contact such person (if they even exist).

meanwhile I'll try uninstalling every thing nvidia-related as well as ironhide to get a fresh start.

Yes... I do see.  Well, this one may be a long one...  I'll explain EDID, your problem and what you might need to do to work-around problems that might arise with media servers.  I have Ubuntu Media Servers with nVidia. 

First, yes, "newer" monitors, TVs, flatscreens, HDMI TVs store hex data inside that contains hex data about their capabilities.  On bootup, Xorg-server initializes the video card driver, then searches through their ports to find devices connected to retrieve their EDID data.  If it doesn't see that on a port, it assumes that nothing is there and shuts off that port.  

So this means if an "older" monitor does not store EDID then the card and driver doesn't see it, then the port gets turned off.  With a media server, you don't always need or have your TV or Display turned on when you boot.... That port, using the normal process, usualluy gets turned off because it doesn't see the device.

Compunding this a little more... There have been some xorg-server changes in the last 2 weeks that have caused Ubuntu users installing new drivers to have problems with EDID.  I've been answering an extraordinary volume of these tissues (other threads) in the past two weeks!  Go thing on this is that I now have some fresh instructions written up for all these workaround in those posts...

**Issue one- Your monitor:**
- What is the date on the label on the back of that monitor?  Model?
- hwinfo = no data and xrandr = data, tells me you have hope...
- "Monitor Settings" probably shows incorrect data for resolutions... (recent Bug)
- NVidia Settings may or may not find your monitor (does it?) and may or may not see the correct resolutions (does it?)
- Even if it does not, if you go to your monitor tab, go to where you can retrieve the EDID Data Button > press > save in your home folder. Start nautilus and maneuver to it > check properties and see if it is more the 0 bytes... It should be 128 bytes.  Don't try to look at it unless you have hex editor.  It is hex data.

- Stop at that point and tell us how it went...  Things branch off on this solution from your answers...

**Issue 2- Media Servers and their Displays:**
Like I think I mentioned above, on a multi-media server, you may have a display on at boot, but you don't always have your TV on at that time.  To force the port to stay on still have the data for your TV, I force the port on and map it to a port, then store a local EDID file for the Video driver to find and bootup and use.  Works out well. The instruction for your problem above amy cover this, so I'll wait on those instructions.

EDIT:
I've used post 280 to install the drivers and configure the xorg.conf, to no avail. starting gdm gives me a black screen, I have to go back to tty1, stop it, delete xorg.conf (or rename xorg.conf.failsafe) and restart it. With that and my xorg.0.log I think it's safe to say my NVIDIA card is DISABLED.
Great right? Not really... But at least I know where I am for sure now.
I haven;t heard back from Jim yet tonight...Jim nor I have a laptop with this hardware. So this is new frontier for us also.  We cannot test ourselves.  We both researched this and found that there is "no instructions" nor troubleshooting guide for this.

What our plan "is" is to guide you two (bring in another than I know has this hardware) install, test and troubleshoot this (which I'm sure will be needed) and put together a guide for "others" to use on this.

Think you might be up for that?  Would benefit you with a working system...,  (note: have a link to that bios hack?)

Now an explanation on where you are at now.  Ironhide says to use nvidia binaries and to install before installing ironhide...

By looking at the source code: What is does basically is when you enable the NVidia card from  within Ironhide, it copies a backup file of the nvidia's xorg.conf file to the /etc/X11/ directory... Disabling copies a generic xorg.conf file and switches the GPU to the Intel.

So, I don't know what it does in the install completely yet... but if it was a generic xorg.conf present, then it probably copied the nvidia xorg.conf to it's storgae/backup directory, replaced it with the Intel generic xorg.conf and switched the GPU to the Intel GPU.  So nothing was wrong there... as far as we know (we could check that) and you probably didn'y need to reinstall.

A reinstall would mean reinstalling Ironhide, which needs those files for it's install.

### Post by lucasfigueiredo on 2011-10-03
> **MAFoElffen said:**
> I haven;t heard back from Jim yet tonight...Jim nor I have a laptop with this hardware. So this is new frontier for us also.  We cannot test ourselves.  We both researched this and found that there is "no instructions" nor troubleshooting guide for this.

What our plan "is" is to guide you two (bring in another than I know has this hardware) install, test and troubleshoot this (which I'm sure will be needed) and put together a guide for "others" to use on this.

Think you might be up for that?  Would benefit you with a working system...,  (note: have a link to that bios hack?)

Now an explanation on where you are at now.  Ironhide says to use nvidia binaries and to install before installing ironhide...

By looking at the source code: What is does basically is when you enable the NVidia card from  within Ironhide, it copies a backup file of the nvidia's xorg.conf file to the /etc/X11/ directory... Disabling copies a generic xorg.conf file and switches the GPU to the Intel.

So, I don't know what it does in the install completely yet... but if it was a generic xorg.conf present, then it probably copied the nvidia xorg.conf to it's storgae/backup directory, replaced it with the Intel generic xorg.conf and switched the GPU to the Intel GPU.  So nothing was wrong there... as far as we know (we could check that) and you probably didn'y need to reinstall.

A reinstall would mean reinstalling Ironhide, which needs those files for it's install.

I feel some explaining is in order:
My Vaio Z has a hardware switch with 3 positions: Speed, Stamina and Auto.
In Windows, whenever I flip the switch to speed, it activates my nvidia card. Stamina activates the Intel and AUTO does that according to whether the laptop is plugged in or not. That's what the internet calls a "Hot Switch".
In Ubuntu the LEDs for that switch don't even come on.
The BIOS hack I found is a common hack, to enable something they call "Advanced Mode". Apparently advanced mode has an option for enabling "Cold Switch", which is a hardware based switch, that enables either card by flipping the switch with the machine turned off.
Using that hack (I haven't really found the one for my BIOS version yet, but I'll post a link below anyway) I would apparently be able to choose the card before booting and, apart from editing xorg.conf, nothing else would be required.
IMO, "Cold Switching" is not a very elegant solution. Ideally I should be able to switch via software, no matter how complicated that may be.
That has not been the case, I have never, under linux, been able to activate my nvidia card.
So I am indeed interested in this project, and will help however I can, but first I think I must successfully activate my nvidia card via ironhide so that we can build up from there.
Is there a way to activate the nvidia card that you know of? And perhaps a better way to test that (I try editing xorg.conf and adding nvidia and it never works).


Thanks again for all your help, I hope I can contribute to this project should it "leave the blueprints" as we (kinda) say in Portuguese. (I'm Brazilian, btw ;))


EDIT: forgot the link
[LINK]("http://feature-enable.blogspot.com/2009/07/enable-vt-on-insydeh2o-based-sony-vaio.html")
I haven't read that, I'll post an update when I have. (I first have to find out what the hell VT means)

Answers:

"VT" are logical X-Session "virtual terminal" sessions.  VT 1 through 6 are TTY Text sessions.  VT 7 is the graphical session that displays the GUI.

>> Is there a way to activate the nvidia card that you know of? 

I used to have a link where you echo'ed commands from the command line to switch GPU's, let me try to find that bookmark...

**NOW WITH TELEVISION CONNECTED:**

```
cat /etc/X11/xorg.conf
# nvidia-xconfig: X configuration file generated by nvidia-xconfig
# nvidia-xconfig:  version 280.13  (buildmeister@swio-display-x86-rhel47-05.nvidia.com)  Wed Jul 27 17:18:55 PDT 2011


Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Layout0"
    Screen      0  "Screen0" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Keyboard0" "CoreKeyboard"
    InputDevice    "Mouse0" "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

    # generated from default
    Identifier     "Mouse0"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "Protocol" "auto"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/psaux"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "no"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

    # generated from default
    Identifier     "Keyboard0"
    Driver         "kbd"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Monitor0"
    VendorName     "Unknown"
    ModelName      "Unknown"
    HorizSync       28.0 - 33.0
    VertRefresh     43.0 - 72.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Device0"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    VendorName     "NVIDIA Corporation"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Device0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
    EndSubSection
EndSection
```

### Post by Blasphemist on 2011-10-03
> **MAFoElffen said:**
> LOL... I'm Portuguese also (both sides from the Azores)... but haven't spoken it since being a little kid.

I'll look into a direct software "switch".  On what you described with the switch and lights, in linux, they don't seem to be close to that yet... Maybe after they get the basics working, then maybe they can get the different makes and models particulars working.

I know it must be frustrating to not be able to use hardware you know is there and works this way...

Answers:

"VT" are logical X-Session "virtual terminal" sessions.  VT 1 through 6 are TTY Text sessions.  VT 7 is the graphical session that displays the GUI.

>> Is there a way to activate the nvidia card that you know of? 

I used to have a link where you echo'ed commands from the command line to switch GPU's, let me try to find that bookmark...
I don't know guys. Some things I see make ironhide seem completely undocumented and some things make it seem like the feeling is not much is needed. But as I find stuff a lot seems taken for granted. Maybe its just me not having this hardware and not being able to get my hands on it but I don't think so. Check this out. [http://askubuntu.com/questions/59489/hybrid-graphics-intel-nvidia](http://askubuntu.com/questions/59489/hybrid-graphics-intel-nvidia)

So far, I just keep coming up with questions. Doesn't that say ironhide installs the card drivers? Martin Juhl plans that you use the xorg edgers ppa. I know I can't answer to someone how to implement ironhide and the right drivers. I'll keep working on getting an understanding and post back more today.

### Post by Blasphemist on 2011-10-03
Almost forgot this. optirun is what I think you're looking for to activate the nvidia for an app. precede the command with optirun to run the app using the nvidia card.

Lucas, did you run ironhide-configuration? Is it hard to understand and/or not documented within the app?

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-10-03
> **MAFoElffen said:**
> >> Is there a way to activate the nvidia card that you know of? 

I used to have a link where you echo'ed commands from the command line to switch GPU's, let me try to find that bookmark...

Here's the one:
[Hybrid graphics Linux on Samsung sf310-S03]("http://linux-hybrid-graphics.blogspot.com/2011/06/hybrid-graphics-linux-on-samsung-sf310.html")
But it echoes thru the apci_call module that is installed by bumblebeel and ironhide...

[acpi_call]("http://hybrid-graphics-linux.tuxfamily.org/index.php?title=Acpi_call") <-- is on installing, configuring and using that particular module alone.

This was an explaination of the pieces, from back in June, before Ironhide.
> 
   [IMG]http://lh3.ggpht.com/-JP4j9xC2sqg/Te9j5xecLbI/AAAAAAAABYY/G7SF-sgrLms/%25255BUNSET%25255D.png[/IMG]
It  was on Feb 2010 that the Hybrid Graphics Linux Launchpad team was  created, and since then the team members have been successful in  bringing decent switchable graphics and GPU offloading capabilities to  Linux with projects like vga_switcheroo, acpi_call or bumblebee:

[LIST]
[*] [Bumblebee]("http://hybrid-graphics-linux.tuxfamily.org/index.php?title=Bumblebee")  -- allows you to run specific programs on the discrete graphic card,   inside of an X session using the integrated graphic card. Works on **Nvidia Optimus** cards -- by Martin Juhl
[*] [asus_switcheroo]("http://hybrid-graphics-linux.tuxfamily.org/index.php?title=Asus_switcheroo") -- a solution for Intel/Nvidia switching on ASUS and other laptops with a similar hardware mux -- by Alex Williamson
[*] [byo_switcheroo]("http://hybrid-graphics-linux.tuxfamily.org/index.php?title=Byo_switcheroo") -- a solution to build your own handler (like acpi_call) to switch between cards with vga_switcheroo -- by Alex Williamson
[*] [vga_switcheroo]("http://hybrid-graphics-linux.tuxfamily.org/index.php?title=Vga_switcheroo") -- the original GPU switching solution primarily for Intel/ATI notebooks -- by David Airlie
[*] [acpi_call]("http://hybrid-graphics-linux.tuxfamily.org/index.php?title=Acpi_call") -- allows you to switch off discrete graphics card to improve battery life -- by Michal Kottman
[*] [PRIME]("http://hybrid-graphics-linux.tuxfamily.org/index.php?title=PRIME") -- long-term Optimus solution in progress -- by David Airlie
[*] [hybrid-windump]("http://hybrid-graphics-linux.tuxfamily.org/index.php?title=Hybrid-windump") -- dump window using Nvidia onto Intel display --  by Florian Berger and Joakim Gebart
[/LIST]




Look down the page to th

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-10-03
> **Blasphemist said:**
> I don't know guys. Some things I see make ironhide seem completely undocumented and some things make it seem like the feeling is not much is needed. But as I find stuff a lot seems taken for granted. Maybe its just me not having this hardware and not being able to get my hands on it but I don't think so. Check this out. [http://askubuntu.com/questions/59489/hybrid-graphics-intel-nvidia](http://askubuntu.com/questions/59489/hybrid-graphics-intel-nvidia)

So far, I just keep coming up with questions. Doesn't that say ironhide installs the card drivers? Martin Juhl plans that you use the xorg edgers ppa. I know I can't answer to someone how to implement ironhide and the right drivers. I'll keep working on getting an understanding and post back more today.
It does seem a real mess.  Even on their blog (and it's archive), they still talk about 3 to 4 methods that are different from ironhide, which some of those separate methods also integrated into ironhide.  No wonder people are confused and lost!  I'm trying to understand it.

Then on the Intel Sandy Bridge Chipsets, one place considers them as the same as Intel... But then others say their not supported.  I think that goes back to the first realease of that chipset and now I think their on their 3rd release or revision.

 A few BIOS vendors had released BIOS update flashes to add the capability switch GPU's, but these are few and for very few brands and models of laptops.

EDIT--
On your questions-
- The vidoe divers have to be installed first... Bumblebee and ironhide does not manage the individual drivers, but rather uses their individual xorg.conf file to start that driver.
- "optirun program_name" is their example is to test with google chrome after you have Bumblebee installed.

---

[    36.792] (==) NVIDIA(0): DPI set to (75, 75); computed from built-in default
[    36.793] (--) Depth 24 pixmap format is 32 bpp

```Finally, collecting EDID info. My Nvidia GUI screen does not offer me the choice to "Acquire EDID..." button like your screenshot. Could it be that this is different with Nvidia 280.13 version of the driver.
The TV is good. You should be able to save the EDID data from that from within nvidia-settings.  

The monitor, even though the nvidia driver says that the checksum of version 1 was invalid,   The bytes there seem valid and it's the right size (128bytes).  The xrandr utility saw it all, so I'm hopeful.  Look at the bottom of the post #310:
> **MAFoElffen said:**
> 
```

Section Device
  #
  Option "CustomEDID" "CRT-0:/tmp/edid1.bin; DFP-0:/tmp/edid2.bin"
  #
EndSection

# --OR--

Section Screen
  Indentifier "CRT-0"
  #
  Option "CustomEDID" "CRT-0:/temp/edid1.bin"
  #
EndSection

```And of course you'ld have to install the package read-edid to be able to  query that data.. But since read-edid would need to be installed to  read that data anyways, you could just use get-edid from that package to  do it..

So it is possible from the commandline.  The read-edid package is a set  of two tools - get-edid, which gets the raw edid information  from the  monitor, and parse-edid, which turns the raw binary information  into an  XF86Config-(xorg.conf) compatible monitor section.  So read-edid will  fecth and create a file of that binary data. parse-edid will parse that  data or file into a human readable format.  Commandline is posibble,  although I don't see any arguments for get-edid to look at multiple  displays... yet:
So from the commandline
```

get-edid > edid1.bin

```You could then use the CustomEDID option to point to that file  we'll go through that step by step after you get the edid.bin files..
We will go over the mod's to the xorg.conf after you are sure you can get the edid.bin from the montior and we'll test that one first.  If the data "IS" invalid, then we can manuall add the modes to the xorg.conf using modelines. (The edid.bin is a savings in work.) The TV you should be able to retrieve easily through nvidia-settings.

### Post by Blasphemist on 2011-10-03
Lucas-

I have some questions but first, you may be the perfect person to help us get a handle on this. You have a hardware switch and can still install if you have to. Let me ask some questions please.
1. What position has the switch been in when you install Ubuntu? Does the switch go to each corner of the triangle? Does it have to be switched while the system is off?
2. When you boot the live CD/USB and choose try Ubuntu, do you get unity or classic gnome? After the install on the initial boot can you successfully load unity?
3. Are you installing natty?
4. What are you doing immediately following the installation? Are you doing all the updates first? 
5. Are you willing to switch to the xorgedgers ppa? (xorg-edgers fresh X crack :D)

Mike- I'm thinking we want to make sure things are done cleanly in this case, since it can be. If he installs with the switch in auto, I'm thinking that both cards should be known during install of ubuntu. Is that right or would the install choose one so to speak? I see Martin Juhl saying ironhide works well with xorg-edgers. I'm thinking that before ironhide is installed and certainly before its configured xorg-edgers ppa should be added. When that isn't possible, I think ironhide would need reconfigured after the ppa and any packages are installed. Can you describe any packages he needs to install when adding that ppa? Or will adding that ppa just make already installed packages have new updates available? Will it matter if he adds this ppa before or after the initial updates following the distro install?

Both of you, do these things make sense and am I missing anything at the base of the pyramid?

### Post by MAFoElffen on 2011-10-03
Quoted from Blasphemist, broke up to answer (at least from what I know about it about this moment...)
> Mike- I'm thinking we want to make sure things are done cleanly in this case, since it can be.Good idea. 
> If he installs with the switch in auto, I'm thinking that both cards should be known during install of ubuntu. Is that right or would the install choose one so to speak?Ironhide will only work with one "basic" style of hybred switched grapghics GPU pairings = Intel / Nvidia that "will" have certain drivers.  The technology for this is only less then 2 years old.  So the drivers for them are the nouveau for Intel (basic graphics, that GPU won't run untiy) and nividia-current styled drivers (270, 260) for the nvidia card.  The Nvidia  "chipset" can be different, but the driver would cover that range.  All these "switch styles", in the install, have logic there that looks for the distribution OS to determine what OS is there, then with that determined Distro, has logic to look for the directory and filename of the files it needs to use... Then should prompt- "Found a solution" 

> I see Martin Juhl saying ironhide works well with xorg-edgers. I'm thinking that before ironhide is installed and certainly before its configured xorg-edgers ppa should be added. When that isn't possible, I think ironhide would need reconfigured after the ppa and any packages are installed. This is okay for now, at this moment in time... This is not always so.  For our tests, we can go for that and leave it until through.

This actually the xorg-edgers ppa that he recommends.  It;s not the main (newest) but rather the archive:
```
